questions.q: An API instance in API Manager has SLA tiers and the SLA-based rate limiting policy applied. What must a consumer do to call the API successfully?
options: Nothing; the SLA rate-limiting policy only monitors and reports on the traffic in the background
Request access in Exchange for a client app + tier, then send its credentials each call
Add their public IP address to the API's allow-list
Deploy and run their own copy of the API instance
questions.explanation: SLA-based policies identify consumers by client credentials issued through the Exchange access-request flow (client app + tier + approved contract). Requests without valid credentials get 401; requests over the tier limit get 429.
optionNotes: Wrong — SLA rate limiting enforces limits, it doesn't merely monitor.
Correct — a client app + approved tier + credentials on each request is required.
Wrong — IP allow-listing is a different policy and doesn't establish a tier.
Wrong — consumers call the managed instance; they don't deploy their own.

questions.q: A Transform Message component receives the payload shown and executes the script. What is the output?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
[
  { "dest": "SFO", "price": 400 },
  { "dest": "LAX", "price": 120 },
  { "dest": "SFO", "price": 900 }
]

SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload filter ($.price > 200) map $.dest
options: ["SFO", "SFO"]
["SFO", "LAX", "SFO"]
[{"dest": "SFO"}, {"dest": "SFO"}]
"SFOSFO"
optionNotes: Correct — filter keeps the 400 and 900 records; map then projects only the dest value of each, producing an array of strings.
Wrong — the filter removes the LAX record (120 is not > 200) before map runs.
Wrong — map returns what the lambda returns; $.dest is the string value, not an object.
Wrong — map never concatenates; it always produces an array with one element per input element.
questions.explanation: Chained array functions evaluate left to right: filter narrows to the two records with price > 200, then map projects $.dest from each, yielding ["SFO", "SFO"].

questions.q: What is the exact structure of the output produced by this script?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
[
  { "code": "A1", "dest": "SFO" },
  { "code": "B2", "dest": "LAX" },
  { "code": "C3", "dest": "SFO" }
]

SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload groupBy $.dest
options: An object with keys "SFO" and "LAX", each holding an ARRAY of the matching flight objects
An array of two arrays, one per destination
An object with keys "SFO" and "LAX", each holding a single flight object
An array of objects each with a 'dest' key and a 'flights' array
optionNotes: Correct — groupBy returns an object keyed by the grouping criteria; each key maps to the array of ALL elements sharing it (SFO has two).
Wrong — groupBy produces an OBJECT, not an array of arrays.
Wrong — each key holds an array even when only one element matches (LAX: array of one).
Wrong — that shape would require a follow-up pluck/map; groupBy alone doesn't produce it.
questions.explanation: groupBy always yields { criteriaValue: [matching elements] }. To convert back to an array you follow it with pluck or mapObject — a favorite exam follow-up.

questions.q: A developer must convert an object of error counts into an array for a report. Which output does this script produce?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
{ "HTTP": 4, "VALIDATION": 9 }

SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload pluck (value, key) -> { type: key, count: value }
options: [{"type": "HTTP", "count": 4}, {"type": "VALIDATION", "count": 9}]
{"type": ["HTTP", "VALIDATION"], "count": [4, 9]}
{"HTTP": 4, "VALIDATION": 9}
[["HTTP", 4], ["VALIDATION", 9]]
optionNotes: Correct — pluck iterates an object's entries and returns an ARRAY of whatever the lambda returns: one {type, count} object per entry.
Wrong — pluck doesn't transpose keys and values into parallel arrays.
Wrong — that's the unchanged input; pluck always converts object → array.
Wrong — the lambda returns objects, not two-element arrays.
questions.explanation: pluck is the object→array converter: for each key/value pair the lambda's result becomes an array element. It is the standard follow-up to groupBy.

questions.q: What does this script output?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var items = [3, 1, 2]
---
items reduce ((item, acc = 10) -> acc + item)
optionNotes: Correct — the accumulator starts at 10 (explicit initial value) and adds 3, 1, 2 → 16.
Wrong — 6 would be the result WITHOUT the initial value (acc starts as the first element).
Wrong — reduce folds to a single value; it never returns an array of intermediate results.
Wrong — the lambda runs for every element; the accumulator doesn't stay at its initial value.
questions.explanation: reduce with an explicit accumulator (acc = 10) folds the array into one value: 10+3+1+2 = 16. Without it, the first element seeds the accumulator.

questions.q: A flow must output XML. The Transform Message uses the script shown. What happens?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
[ { "id": 1 }, { "id": 2 } ]

SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
items: payload map item: { id: $.id }
options: It fails / invalid XML — the array must be wrapped as repeated elements with {( ... )}
It outputs <items><item><id>1</id></item><item><id>2</id></item></items> correctly
It outputs a JSON array because the input was JSON
It outputs only the first item
optionNotes: Correct — assigning an ARRAY to an XML key is invalid; the idiom is items: {( payload map item: {...} )} which spreads the array into repeated 'item' elements.
Wrong — that's the DESIRED output, but it requires the {( ... )} dynamic-elements syntax around the map.
Wrong — the output directive (application/xml) controls the format regardless of input format.
Wrong — DataWeave doesn't silently truncate arrays.
questions.explanation: XML has no array concept — repeated elements must be created by spreading an array of key:value pairs with {( ... )}: items: {( payload map item: { id: $.id } )}.

questions.q: Given the XML payload and script shown, what is the output?
questions.exhibit: INPUT payload (application/xml): A1 B2 SCRIPT: %dw 2.0 output application/json --- payload.flights.*flight.code
options: null
optionNotes: Correct — .*flight selects ALL repeated flight elements as an array; .code then projects each one's code value.
Wrong — a single-value selector (payload.flights.flight.code) would return only the FIRST match; .* returns all.
Wrong — selectors return values, not wrapping objects.
Wrong — the path exists; null would mean no match.
questions.explanation: The multi-value selector .* is essential for repeated XML elements: payload.flights.*flight is an array of both flights, and .code maps over it.

questions.q: A response must show 'Anonymous' when the optional customer name is absent. Which script fragment is correct?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
{ "order": 1 }   // note: no 'name' field
options: payload.name default "Anonymous"
payload.name orElse "Anonymous"
if payload.name == null "Anonymous"
payload.name ?? "Anonymous"
optionNotes: Correct — the default operator substitutes the right side when the left evaluates to null.
Wrong — orElse is not a DataWeave operator (it exists in other languages/Java Optionals).
Wrong — DataWeave's if requires an else branch; this is a syntax error.
Wrong — ?? is not DataWeave syntax; the null-coalescing operator here is 'default'.
questions.explanation: DataWeave's null fallback is the default operator: expression default fallbackValue. Missing keys evaluate to null, triggering the fallback.

questions.q: What does this script output?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var prices = [100, 300, 200]
---
(prices orderBy $)[-1 to 0]
optionNotes: Correct — orderBy sorts ascending ([100,200,300]); the range [-1 to 0] indexes from the last element back to the first, reversing to descending.
Wrong — that's the ascending intermediate result before the [-1 to 0] reversal.
Wrong — [-1 to 0] is a RANGE selection of all elements in reverse, not just the last one ([-1] alone would give 300).
Wrong — [0] would give the first element; the range covers all elements reversed.
questions.explanation: DataWeave's orderBy only sorts ascending; the standard descending idiom is to select the reversed range: (array orderBy criteria)[-1 to 0].

questions.q: A CSV payload must be produced from JSON, using ';' as separator and no header row. Which output directive is correct?
options: output application/csv separator=";", header=false
output application/csv with { separator: ";", header: false }
output text/csv --separator=';' --no-header
output application/csv (the properties can only be set on the reader)
optionNotes: Correct — writer properties are appended to the output directive: output application/csv separator=";", header=false.
Wrong — 'with { }' is not the syntax for format properties.
Wrong — CLI-style flags are not DataWeave syntax.
Wrong — both readers AND writers accept format properties; writer properties go on the output line.
questions.explanation: Reader/writer properties attach to the format declaration: e.g. output application/csv separator=";", header=false. The same pattern configures readers (input directive or connector MIME type parameters).

questions.q: What is the output of this script?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ a: 1 } ++ { b: 2 }
options: A type error — ++ only works on strings and arrays
optionNotes: Correct — ++ concatenates objects by merging their key/value pairs.
Wrong — ++ on two objects produces one object, not an array.
Wrong — ++ never adds values; it appends entries.
Wrong — ++ is overloaded for strings, arrays, AND objects (and date/time composition).
questions.explanation: ++ concatenates: strings ('a'++'b'), arrays ([1]++[2]), and objects ({a:1}++{b:2} = {a:1,b:2}) — a frequent exam identification question.

questions.q: The API returns dates as 'dd-MM-yyyy' strings but the target system needs ISO 'yyyy-MM-dd'. The input field is payload.bookingDate = "05-03-2026". Which expression produces "2026-03-05"?
options: (payload.bookingDate as Date {format: "dd-MM-yyyy"}) as String {format: "yyyy-MM-dd"}
payload.bookingDate as Date as String
payload.bookingDate replace "-" with "/"
(payload.bookingDate as String {format: "yyyy-MM-dd"}) as Date
optionNotes: Correct — parse the non-ISO string by declaring ITS format, then format the resulting Date with the target pattern.
Wrong — a bare 'as Date' expects ISO-8601 input; "05-03-2026" fails to parse without an explicit format.
Wrong — replace would give "05/03/2026": still the wrong field order, and no date semantics.
Wrong — the coercion order is reversed: you can't format a String 'as String {format}' meaningfully before parsing it into a Date.
questions.explanation: Date reformatting is always parse-then-format: as Date {format: <input pattern>} followed by as String {format: <output pattern>}.

questions.q: What does this script return?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var data = [ { "id": 1 }, { "id": 2 }, { "id": 1 } ]
---
data distinctBy $.id
optionNotes: Correct — distinctBy keeps the FIRST occurrence of each unique criteria value, preserving the whole element.
Wrong — elements that appeared once are kept too; only later duplicates are dropped.
Wrong — distinctBy returns the original ELEMENTS, not the criteria values.
Wrong — that would be a count aggregation; distinctBy doesn't count.
questions.explanation: distinctBy de-duplicates an array by the given criteria, returning the original elements with later duplicates removed.

questions.q: Which output does this produce?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"ER38sd,ER45if,ER99xx" splitBy "," map ($ ++ "!")
options: "ER38sd!,ER45if!,ER99xx!"
["ER38sd,ER45if,ER99xx!"]
An error — map cannot follow splitBy
optionNotes: Correct — splitBy yields the array of codes; map appends '!' to each string element.
Wrong — the result stays an array; joinBy would be needed to rebuild a single string.
Wrong — splitBy splits on EVERY comma, producing three elements.
Wrong — splitBy returns an Array, and map operates on arrays; the chain is idiomatic.
questions.explanation: splitBy: String → Array; map transforms each element; joinBy would convert back. Knowing which functions produce which types is half of DataWeave questions.

questions.q: The script defines and uses a custom function. What is the output?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
fun label(p) = if (p > 500) "premium" else "standard"
---
[600, 100] map { price: $, tier: label($) }
options: [{"price": 600, "tier": "premium"}, {"price": 100, "tier": "standard"}]
[{"price": 600, "tier": "standard"}, {"price": 100, "tier": "premium"}]
{"price": [600, 100], "tier": ["premium", "standard"]}
An error — functions cannot be called inside map
optionNotes: Correct — 600 > 500 → premium; 100 → standard; map builds one object per element.
Wrong — the condition is p > 500; the tiers are swapped here.
Wrong — map produces an array of per-element objects, not parallel arrays.
Wrong — header-declared functions are callable anywhere in the body, including lambdas.
questions.explanation: Header fun declarations are ordinary functions; inside map, $ is each element, so label($) evaluates per element. Trace the conditional carefully — swapped-branch distractors are common.

questions.q: What does the descendants selector return here?
options: An error — .. is not valid
optionNotes: Correct — payload..price collects EVERY price at any depth ([10, 5]); sum() adds them to 15.
Wrong — that's the intermediate array BEFORE sum() is applied.
Wrong — .. finds all matches at any level, not just the first.
Wrong — .. is the descendants selector, fully valid DataWeave.
questions.explanation: The descendants selector .. traverses the whole structure collecting every matching key. Wrapped in sum(), the array [10, 5] becomes 15.

questions.q: In the expression 'payload map (item, index) -> ...', what do the shorthand variables $ and $$ correspond to?
options: $ = item (current value), $$ = index
$ = index, $$ = item
$ = payload, $$ = attributes
$ = the previous element, $$ = the next element
optionNotes: Correct — for arrays, $ is the current element and $$ its index (named 'item, index' when declared explicitly).
Wrong — reversed; this is the most common DataWeave misreading.
Wrong — $ and $$ are lambda parameters, unrelated to the event structure.
Wrong — DataWeave lambdas don't expose neighboring elements.
questions.explanation: Arrays: $ = value, $$ = index. (For object functions like mapObject/pluck, $ = value and $$ = key.) Memorize both pairs.

questions.q: The Transform must uppercase all KEYS while leaving values untouched. Which script is correct for input { "code": "a1", "dest": "sfo" }?
options:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::core::Strings
---
payload mapObject (v, k) -> { (upper(k)): v }

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload map (v, k) -> { upper(k): v }

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
upper(payload)

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload pluck (v, k) -> { (upper(k)): v }
optionNotes: Correct — mapObject transforms entries of an OBJECT; (upper(k)) with parentheses evaluates the expression as a dynamic key. upper comes from dw::core::Strings.
Wrong — map is for ARRAYS; on an object it fails. Also the dynamic key needs parentheses.
Wrong — upper works on a String, not an entire object.
Wrong — pluck returns an ARRAY of the lambda results; the output would be [ {CODE: a1}, {DEST: sfo} ], not one object.
questions.explanation: mapObject keeps object→object; dynamic key names must be wrapped in parentheses ((upper(k)): v). pluck is the object→array variant — a deliberate trap here.

questions.q: What is the value of the 'result' field?
questions.exhibit:
SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var vals = [1, 2, 3, 4]
---
{ result: sizeOf(vals filter (($ mod 2) == 0)) }
optionNotes: Correct — the filter keeps even numbers [2, 4]; sizeOf counts them → 2.
Wrong — 4 is the size of the ORIGINAL array; the filter runs first.
Wrong — [2, 4] is the filtered array before sizeOf is applied.
Wrong — 6 would be sum([2,4]); the script asks for size, not sum.
questions.explanation: Evaluate inside-out: filter → [2, 4]; sizeOf → 2. Distractors are always the intermediate values — track which function is outermost.

questions.q: A reusable DataWeave library file 'Utils.dwl' is saved in src/main/resources/modules with a function 'toCode'. How does a Transform script use it?
questions.exhibit:
src/main/resources/modules/Utils.dwl:
%dw 2.0
fun toCode(s) = upper(s[0 to 2])
options: import modules::Utils --- then call Utils::toCode(payload.name)
include "modules/Utils.dwl" --- then call toCode(payload.name)
lookup("Utils", payload.name)
%dw imports are not supported; the function must be copied into each script
optionNotes: Correct — libraries under src/main/resources import by path with :: separators; qualified calls use Module::function (or import * from modules::Utils for unqualified calls).
Wrong — 'include' is not DataWeave syntax.
Wrong — lookup() invokes Mule FLOWS, not DataWeave modules.
Wrong — module imports are a core DataWeave feature.
questions.explanation: DW modules live under src/main/resources and are imported with import modules::Utils (or import * from ...). lookup() is unrelated — it calls flows.

questions.q: What does this match expression output for the given payload?
questions.exhibit:
INPUT payload (application/json):
{ "status": 404 }

SCRIPT:
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload.status match {
  case 200 -> "ok"
  case is Number -> "other number"
  case s if (s == 404) -> "not found"
  else -> "unknown"
}
options: "other number"
"not found"
"ok"
"unknown"
optionNotes: Correct — cases evaluate IN ORDER: 404 ≠ 200, but 'is Number' matches first, so its branch wins before the more specific 404 guard is reached.
Wrong — the 404 guard is declared AFTER 'is Number'; match stops at the first matching case.
Wrong — a case matched, so the else never runs.
questions.explanation: match/case evaluates top-down and stops at the FIRST match — putting a broad type case before a specific value case shadows it, exactly like Choice router ordering.

questions.q:
A project team must deliver these components:
1. An API that retrieves inventory levels from SAP
2. An API that combines inventory and pricing to build product availability
3. An API returning availability formatted for the point-of-sale devices

Assign each to the correct API-led layer.
options: 1 = System, 2 = Process, 3 = Experience
1 = Process, 2 = System, 3 = Experience
1 = System, 2 = Experience, 3 = Process
1 = Experience, 2 = Process, 3 = System
optionNotes: Correct — unlocking one system of record = System; combining/orchestrating = Process; channel-specific formatting (POS devices) = Experience.
Wrong — retrieving from a single backend (SAP) is the System layer's definition, not Process.
Wrong — combining data across sources is orchestration (Process), not channel adaptation.
Wrong — completely inverted: the SAP unlock is the bottom layer, the POS formatting the top.
questions.explanation: Layer assignment follows the verbs: unlock a system → System API; orchestrate/combine → Process API; format for one channel → Experience API.

questions.q: A developer needs to: (1) write a RAML specification collaboratively, (2) let consumers try the API before implementation, and (3) find a reusable 'Address' data type published by another team. Which Anypoint Platform components serve each need?
options: 1 = Design Center (API Designer), 2 = the mocking service, 3 = Anypoint Exchange
1 = Runtime Manager, 2 = API Manager, 3 = Design Center
1 = Exchange, 2 = Runtime Manager, 3 = the mocking service
1 = Anypoint Studio only, 2 = API Manager, 3 = Access Management
optionNotes: Correct — specs are authored in Design Center's API Designer, simulated by the mocking service, and reusable fragments are discovered in Exchange.
Wrong — Runtime Manager deploys/monitors apps; API Manager governs deployed APIs — neither authors specs.
Wrong — Exchange catalogs assets; it isn't the authoring editor, and Runtime Manager can't simulate an unimplemented API.
Wrong — Studio implements; Access Management handles users/permissions.
questions.explanation: Design-time trio: API Designer (write) → mocking service (simulate) → Exchange (share/discover). Management components (API/Runtime Manager) come after implementation.

questions.q: An organization measures its C4E quarterly. Which metric BEST indicates the application network is delivering its promised value?
options: The percentage of new projects that consume at least one existing API from Exchange
The total number of Mule applications deployed
The number of integrations built by the C4E team itself
The number of vCores purchased
optionNotes: Correct — the application network's value thesis is REUSE; projects consuming existing assets instead of rebuilding is the direct measure.
Wrong — app count measures activity/cost, not reuse or acceleration.
Wrong — a C4E that builds everything itself is a bottleneck (CoE anti-pattern), not a success signal.
Wrong — capacity purchased measures spend, not value.
questions.explanation: C4E KPIs are consumption-based: asset reuse rate, consumers per API, time-to-first-API. Production-side numbers don't demonstrate network effects.

questions.q: APIkit scaffolds flows from the RAML shown. Which flow names are generated for the /orders resource?
questions.exhibit:
#%RAML 1.0
title: Orders API

/orders:
  get:
  post:
    body:
      application/json:
  /{orderId}:
    get:
options: get:\orders:orders-api-config, post:\orders:application\json:orders-api-config, get:\orders\(orderId):orders-api-config
getOrders, postOrders, getOrderById
orders-get, orders-post, orders-orderId-get
get:/orders, post:/orders, get:/orders/{orderId} — the RAML paths, not the APIkit flow names
optionNotes: Correct — the convention is method:\resource[:mediaType]:configName; POST includes the media type because a body is declared, and {orderId} becomes (orderId).
Wrong — camelCase names are not the convention; the router matches on the exact generated pattern.
Wrong — that naming doesn't include the config name and wouldn't be routable.
Wrong — close, but flow names use backslashes, parentheses for URI params, and end with the config name.
questions.explanation: APIkit routing is name-based: method:\resource\(uriParam):mediaType:configName. Recognizing valid generated names (and why renaming breaks routing) is a staple exam topic.

questions.q:
Clients call an APIkit-based API. Match each request to the response, given the RAML exhibit.

Request 1: GET /api/flights?dest=BOS
Request 2: DELETE /api/flights
Request 3: GET /api/pilots
questions.exhibit:
#%RAML 1.0
title: Flights

/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      dest:
        enum: [SFO, LAX]
        required: false
options: 1 → 400 Bad Request; 2 → 405 Method Not Allowed; 3 → 404 Not Found
All three → 501 Not Implemented
optionNotes: Correct — BOS violates the enum → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST (400); DELETE isn't declared on an EXISTING resource → METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED (405); /pilots isn't in the spec at all → NOT_FOUND (404).
Wrong — the router validates enums; dest=BOS cannot reach the flow with a 200.
Wrong — 2 and 3 are swapped: unknown METHOD on a known resource = 405, unknown RESOURCE = 404.
Wrong — 501 is raised when a resource exists in the spec but its implementation flow is missing — not the case in these three requests.
questions.explanation: The APIkit router's validation map: contract violation → 400; undeclared method on declared resource → 405; undeclared resource → 404; declared but unimplemented → 501.

questions.q: After importing a RAML from Exchange into Studio and scaffolding, a developer notices a flow with an HTTP Listener on path /console/*. What is its purpose?
options: It serves the interactive API console generated from the RAML
It is the main routing flow for all API traffic
It exposes JMX metrics
It is a leftover that should always be deleted before deployment
optionNotes: Correct — the console flow serves live, interactive documentation rendered from the spec.
Wrong — API traffic goes through the main flow (default path /api/*) with the APIkit Router.
Wrong — metrics are not exposed by APIkit consoles.
Wrong — it's optional in production (often disabled), but its purpose is the interactive console, not garbage.
questions.explanation: Scaffolding generates: the main flow (listener + APIkit router + error handlers), one flow per method/resource, and a console flow on /console/* for interactive docs.

questions.q: The RAML adds a new resource /refunds after the implementation was already built. What is the correct way to update the Mule project?
options: Right-click the API in Studio → Scaffold flows; existing flows are preserved
Delete all flows and re-import the RAML from scratch
Manually create a flow named refunds-flow
Nothing — APIkit picks up spec changes at runtime and adds the flows automatically
optionNotes: Correct — re-scaffolding is additive: it generates flows for new resources/methods without touching implemented ones.
Wrong — destroying implemented flows loses all work; never necessary.
Wrong — a hand-made name that doesn't follow the get:\refunds:config convention will never be routed to.
Wrong — scaffolding is a design-time action; the router needs the generated flow to exist.
questions.explanation: Keeping spec and implementation in sync = re-scaffold after spec changes. The generator only adds missing flows; the naming convention connects them to the router.

questions.q: An APIkit implementation flow ends with just a Transform Message setting the payload. How does the HTTP response status get set to 201 for the POST flow, per the RAML's declared 201 response?
options: Set an httpStatus variable or the listener's response status field; otherwise it returns 200
APIkit reads the RAML and automatically returns 201 Created for every POST implementation flow
The Transform must output a special __status field in the payload
Status codes cannot be customized with APIkit
optionNotes: Correct — scaffolded projects wire the listener's response status to a variable (commonly vars.httpStatus); the flow sets it to 201. Default success status is 200.
Wrong — the spec DOCUMENTS 201, but the implementation must actually set it; APIkit doesn't infer runtime status from the RAML.
Wrong — payload fields don't drive the status line.
Wrong — they can: via the listener's response builder / status code expression.
questions.explanation: HTTP status is controlled by the listener's response configuration — scaffolding binds it to a variable so each APIkit flow can set its declared status (e.g., 201 for creation).

questions.q: A request GET /api/flights/ABC arrives, where the RAML declares /flights/{ID} with ID of type integer. The implementation flow exists. What happens?
options: APIkit rejects it with APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST (400); the URI param fails type validation
The flow runs and receives attributes.uriParams.ID = "ABC"
The router returns 404 Not Found because flight ABC does not exist in the backend database
The router converts ABC to 0 and continues
optionNotes: Correct — URI parameters are validated against their declared RAML type; a non-integer value violates the contract → 400.
Wrong — validation happens BEFORE routing; the flow is never invoked with an invalid parameter.
Wrong — 404-by-lookup would be the FLOW's business decision; the router 404s only for undeclared resources. Here validation fails first with 400.
Wrong — the router never coerces invalid values silently.
questions.explanation: The router enforces the full contract — path shape, methods, query params, headers, body, AND URI parameter types — rejecting violations as APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST before any flow logic runs.

questions.q: A team hand-builds a RESTful interface with one HTTP Listener per resource instead of using APIkit. Which consequences should they expect? (Select all that apply)
options: Request validation against the API spec must be implemented manually
Nothing keeps the implementation aligned with the published RAML over time
Standard error responses (400/404/405) must be hand-coded
The application cannot be deployed to CloudHub
optionNotes: Correct — without the router, parameter/body/type validation is custom code.
Correct — spec drift is undetected; APIkit's scaffolding/validation is what enforces the contract.
Correct — APIkit's generated error handler provides the standard mappings; manual interfaces must reproduce them.
Wrong — deployment targets are unrelated to how the interface is built.
questions.explanation: Hand-built interfaces work but forfeit APIkit's three gifts: automatic contract validation, standardized error responses, and spec-implementation synchronization.

questions.q: What does the APIkit Router populate for the implementation flow when routing GET /api/flights/42?dest=SFO (RAML declares /flights/{ID} with query param dest)?
options: attributes.uriParams.ID = "42" and attributes.queryParams.dest = "SFO"
vars.ID = 42 and vars.dest = "SFO"
payload.ID and payload.dest
Nothing — the flow must parse the raw URL
optionNotes: Correct — the router validates and populates uriParams/queryParams in the message attributes.
Wrong — parameters arrive as attributes, not variables.
Wrong — the payload holds the request BODY (empty for this GET), never the URL parts.
Wrong — parsing is exactly what the router does for you.
questions.explanation: After APIkit routing, the flow reads attributes.uriParams.<name> and attributes.queryParams.<name> — same attributes structure as any HTTP Listener event.

questions.q: Trace this flow. What are the payload and vars.original AFTER the HTTP Request completes?
questions.exhibit:
FLOW (pseudo-XML):
<flow name="orders">
  <http:listener path="/orders"/>          <!-- request body: {"id": 7} -->
  <set-variable variableName="original" value="#[payload]"/>
  <http:request method="GET" path="/inventory"/>   <!-- responds: {"stock": 3} -->
</flow>
options: payload = {"stock": 3}; vars.original = {"id": 7}
payload = {"id": 7}; vars.original = {"id": 7}
payload = {"stock": 3}; vars.original = {"stock": 3}
payload = {"id": 7}; vars.original = {"stock": 3}
optionNotes: Correct — the HTTP Request replaces the payload with its response, but variables survive operations unchanged.
Wrong — without a target, the operation ALWAYS replaces the payload with the response body.
Wrong — variables are not re-evaluated when the payload changes; original captured {"id": 7} at set time.
Wrong — both halves inverted.
questions.explanation: Connector operations replace the MESSAGE (payload + attributes); variables persist for the rest of the flow. Saving the payload to a var before an operation is the standard pattern to keep it.

questions.q: The same flow is changed: the HTTP Request now has target="inventory" and targetValue="#[payload.stock]". What are payload and vars.inventory after it completes?
questions.exhibit:
<http:request method="GET" path="/inventory"
              target="inventory"
              targetValue="#[payload.stock]"/>
<!-- request body before the call: {"id": 7} -->
<!-- HTTP response: {"stock": 3} -->
options: payload = {"id": 7} (unchanged); vars.inventory = 3
payload = {"stock": 3}; vars.inventory = 3
payload = {"id": 7}; vars.inventory = {"stock": 3}
payload = 3; vars.inventory = {"id": 7}
optionNotes: Correct — with a target, the message is NOT replaced; targetValue (evaluated against the operation's RESULT) picks payload.stock = 3 into the variable.
Wrong — target redirects the result into a variable precisely so the payload stays untouched.
Wrong — targetValue #[payload.stock] extracts the number 3; without targetValue the default #[payload] would store the whole object.
Wrong — the flow's payload is never replaced when target is set.
questions.explanation: target/targetValue = message enrichment: the operation result (or a fragment of it, via targetValue evaluated on that result) lands in a variable while the in-flight payload is preserved.

questions.q: A client calls: POST /api/track?mode=fast with header client_id: abc123 and body {"pkg": 9}. Which expressions read the mode, the client_id, and the pkg values?
options: attributes.queryParams.mode · attributes.headers.client_id · payload.pkg
attributes.uriParams.mode · attributes.client_id · payload.pkg
payload.mode · attributes.headers.client_id · attributes.body.pkg
vars.mode · vars.client_id · vars.pkg
optionNotes: Correct — query string → attributes.queryParams; request headers → attributes.headers; the body IS the payload.
Wrong — uriParams holds path template values like /track/{id}; mode came from the query string, and headers live under attributes.headers.
Wrong — the body is the payload itself, not attributes.body; mode is not in the body.
Wrong — nothing populates variables automatically; vars only hold what the flow sets.
questions.explanation: HTTP Listener event map: body → payload; query string → attributes.queryParams; path templates → attributes.uriParams; headers → attributes.headers; method/path → attributes.method/requestPath.

questions.q: A Logger placed after a Database SELECT logs #[attributes]. What does it show?
options: Database result metadata; the Select replaced the listener attributes
The original HTTP Listener attributes (headers, queryParams), still present unchanged
Both HTTP and Database attributes merged
null — operations clear attributes
optionNotes: Correct — every operation REPLACES the message, attributes included; after Select they describe the DB operation's result.
Wrong — those were replaced the moment the Select completed; retrieving them later requires having saved them to a variable.
Wrong — attributes are replaced wholesale, never merged.
Wrong — they're replaced with the operation's metadata, not nulled.
questions.explanation: The golden rule: after ANY connector operation, payload AND attributes belong to that operation. Needing pre-operation attributes later = save them to a var first.

questions.q: Which component sets the variable 'total' to the sum of payload.items[*].price using a full DataWeave script, while ALSO being able to change the payload in the same step?
options: Transform Message — it can set multiple outputs: the payload and any variables
Set Variable — it can also transform and replace the current payload at the same time
Set Payload — it also sets variables
Logger with a target
optionNotes: Correct — Transform Message supports several simultaneous outputs (payload, vars, attributes), each with its own DataWeave script.
Wrong — Set Variable writes exactly one variable and leaves the payload alone.
Wrong — Set Payload writes only the payload.
Wrong — Logger writes to the log; it has no target parameter.
questions.explanation: Transform Message is the multi-output transformation tool; Set Variable/Set Payload are single-purpose setters for simple values.

questions.q: Flow A sets vars.customer and then calls Flow B via Flow Reference. Flow B modifies the payload and sets vars.status. What does Flow A see after the Flow Reference returns?
options: The payload modified by B, vars.customer intact, AND the new vars.status
Its own original payload; only vars.customer
B's payload; vars are reset to empty
Nothing changes — Flow Reference passes a copy
optionNotes: Correct — Flow Reference passes the WHOLE event synchronously; all changes (payload and variables) return to the caller.
Wrong — the payload change made in B persists in A after the reference.
Wrong — variables are part of the traveling event; they're never reset by a Flow Reference.
Wrong — a COPY is what Async scope gets; Flow Reference shares the event.
questions.explanation: Within one application, Flow Reference is a synchronous call sharing the full event — contrast with the Async scope (copy, changes lost) and HTTP between apps (only payload crosses).

questions.q: The same logic is moved into an Async scope. Flow A sets vars.status='pending', the Async scope's processors set vars.status='done' and change the payload. What does Flow A see immediately after the Async scope?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="a">
  <set-variable variableName="status" value="pending"/>
  <async>
    <set-variable variableName="status" value="done"/>
    <set-payload value="processed"/>
  </async>
  <logger message="#[vars.status ++ ' / ' ++ payload]"/>
</flow>
options: vars.status='pending', original payload — the async branch ran on a copy
vars.status = 'done' and payload = 'processed'
The flow blocks until the async branch finishes, then sees 'done'
A runtime error — variables cannot be set inside Async
optionNotes: Correct — Async hands a copy of the event to another thread and returns immediately; its changes never come back.
Wrong — that would be the Flow Reference behavior; Async changes are isolated.
Wrong — not waiting is the entire point of the Async scope.
Wrong — anything can run inside Async; the changes just stay in the copy.
questions.explanation: Async = fire-and-forget on an event COPY: the main flow proceeds immediately and never observes the branch's modifications. Use it for logging/notifications that must not delay the response.

questions.q: In a Logger's message field, which syntax embeds the value of the query parameter 'dest' in a text message?
options: #['Flying to ' ++ attributes.queryParams.dest]
${attributes.queryParams.dest}
{{attributes.queryParams.dest}}
%attributes.queryParams.dest%
optionNotes: Correct — #[...] marks an inline DataWeave expression; ++ concatenates the strings.
Wrong — ${...} is PROPERTY placeholder syntax, resolved from configuration files at startup, not runtime event data.
Wrong — mustache-style syntax is not Mule expression syntax.
Wrong — percent delimiters are not Mule expression syntax.
questions.explanation: Two different syntaxes coexist: #[dataweave] evaluates per event at runtime; ${property} resolves configuration values at deployment. Mixing them up is a favorite trick question.

questions.q: A flow receives JSON but the payload must be sent to a legacy component as a Java LinkedHashMap. What accomplishes the conversion?
options: A Transform Message with output application/java
Setting the listener's MIME type to application/java
Java conversion happens automatically for all payloads
A Set Payload with value #[payload]
optionNotes: Correct — output application/java materializes the structure as Java objects (maps/lists).
Wrong — the listener's MIME type declares how to READ incoming data, and the incoming data is JSON.
Wrong — Mule 4 keeps data in its reader format until a transform requests another; conversion is explicit.
Wrong — that re-sets the same payload without changing its format.
questions.explanation: Format conversions are Transform Message output directives: application/json ↔ application/java ↔ application/xml etc.

questions.q: What is the correlation ID and when is it assigned?
options: A unique id set when the source creates the event, carried through the flow
A random value that every processor regenerates as the event passes through it
The HTTP session cookie
An ID the developer must always set manually
optionNotes: Correct — every event gets a correlation ID at creation; it appears in logs and propagates (X-Correlation-ID) across the HTTP connector.
Wrong — it stays STABLE through the event's journey; that's what makes it useful for tracing.
Wrong — unrelated to cookies or sessions.
Wrong — it's automatic; developers may OVERRIDE it (e.g., with a business key) but never need to create it.
questions.explanation: The correlation ID is the event's tracing thread: assigned at the source, stable through processors, logged by default patterns, propagated to downstream apps.

questions.q: A flow must respond to the client with the ORIGINAL request body even though it calls two enrichment services. Which design achieves this most cleanly?
options: Call both services with target variables (e.g., target="svc1", target="svc2") so the payload never changes
Call the services normally and re-fetch the original body from attributes at the end
Use an Async scope for both calls
Store the payload in an Object Store and read it back
optionNotes: Correct — target variables are message enrichment: results land in vars, the request payload flows untouched to the response.
Wrong — the original body is the PAYLOAD, not an attribute, and it was replaced by the first call.
Wrong — Async loses the responses entirely (copy semantics) — the enrichment data would be unavailable.
Wrong — persistent storage for an in-flight value is heavyweight misuse; a variable (or target) is the tool.
questions.explanation: When the response must be the original payload, enrich via target variables — the canonical Mule 4 replacement for Mule 3's message enricher.

questions.q: Which statement about Mule event immutability is TRUE?
options: Each processor produces a NEW event; components never modify the event in place
The payload can never change during a flow
Variables are immutable once set
Immutability means the event and its data cannot be passed across flow boundaries
optionNotes: Correct — 'changes' create new immutable instances behind the scenes; each processor receives the previous processor's output event.
Wrong — the payload changes constantly (transforms, operations); immutability is about INSTANCES, not values over time.
Wrong — variables can be overwritten or removed at any point.
Wrong — events travel through Flow References freely.
questions.explanation: Immutability is an implementation guarantee: processors receive and emit event instances rather than mutating shared state — enabling safe parallelism (e.g., Scatter-Gather copies).

questions.q: Trace this flow when the HTTP Request throws HTTP:NOT_FOUND. What response does the CLIENT receive?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="main">
  <http:listener path="/data"/>
  <http:request path="/backend"/>          <!-- throws HTTP:NOT_FOUND -->
  <set-payload value='{"ok": true}'/>
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-continue type="HTTP:NOT_FOUND">
      <set-payload value='{"fallback": true}'/>
    </on-error-continue>
  </error-handler>
</flow>
options: HTTP 200 with body {"fallback": true}
HTTP 500 with body {"fallback": true}
HTTP 200 with body {"ok": true}
HTTP 404 with the backend's error body
optionNotes: Correct — On Error Continue treats the flow as SUCCESSFUL: the handler's payload becomes the response with a success (200) status.
Wrong — 500 is the Propagate/default-handler outcome; Continue converts the error into success.
Wrong — the set-payload after the failing processor is SKIPPED; execution jumped to the handler.
Wrong — the backend's 404 was consumed by the handler; the client sees the handled result.
questions.explanation: On Error Continue: handler output → success response (200). Remember the skipped remainder: processors after the failing one never run.

questions.q: Same flow, but the handler is on-error-propagate instead. What does the client receive?
questions.exhibit:
<error-handler>
  <on-error-propagate type="HTTP:NOT_FOUND">
    <set-payload value='{"fallback": true}'/>
  </on-error-propagate>
</error-handler>
options: HTTP 500 with body {"fallback": true}
HTTP 200 with body {"fallback": true}
HTTP 404 forwarded from the backend
No response — the connection is dropped
optionNotes: Correct — Propagate runs its processors (so the BODY is the fallback payload) but re-throws, so the listener returns its ERROR response — 500 by default.
Wrong — 200 is the Continue outcome; Propagate keeps the flow failed.
Wrong — the listener doesn't forward the backend's status; the flow's error response applies (customizable on the listener's error response builder).
Wrong — the listener always answers; error responses are still responses.
questions.explanation: Propagate = handled-but-still-failed: handler processors shape the error body, then the error continues up, producing the listener's error response (default 500).

questions.q: Flow 'parent' calls flow 'child' via Flow Reference. child's error handler has on-error-continue for VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL. A validator inside child throws exactly that error. What happens in parent?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="parent">
  <flow-ref name="child"/>
  <logger message="after child"/>
</flow>

<flow name="child">
  <validation:is-email email="#[payload.email]"/>   <!-- throws -->
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-continue type="VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL">
      <set-payload value="invalid-mail-handled"/>
    </on-error-continue>
  </error-handler>
</flow>
options: parent continues normally: the logger runs, payload is "invalid-mail-handled"
parent's error handling triggers at the Flow Reference
parent stops silently without logging
The error escapes to the default error handler and returns 500
optionNotes: Correct — child handled the error with Continue, so the Flow Reference call SUCCEEDS; the handler's payload returns to parent.
Wrong — that's the on-error-PROPAGATE outcome: only a re-thrown error surfaces at the Flow Reference.
Wrong — nothing stops; the call completed successfully from parent's perspective.
Wrong — the error was fully consumed inside child.
questions.explanation: Continue in the CALLED flow makes the Flow Reference succeed; Propagate would resurface the error in the caller. This caller/callee interaction is among the most-tested error scenarios.

questions.q: An error handler declares these scopes IN ORDER. Which scope handles a DB:CONNECTIVITY error?
questions.exhibit:
<error-handler>
  <on-error-continue type="MULE:ANY">          <!-- scope 1 -->
  <on-error-propagate type="DB:CONNECTIVITY">  <!-- scope 2 -->
</error-handler>
options: Scope 1 — MULE:ANY matches first, top-down
Scope 2 — the more specific type always wins
Both scopes execute in order
Neither — DB errors need a dedicated handler flow
optionNotes: Correct — evaluation is strictly ordered; ANY placed first shadows every scope below it.
Wrong — there is NO specificity ranking; only declaration order matters.
Wrong — exactly one scope handles an error.
Wrong — DB:CONNECTIVITY is an ordinary matchable error type.
questions.explanation: First-match-wins with no specificity logic: always order specific types BEFORE general ones; MULE:ANY (if used) goes last.

questions.q: A Try scope wraps only the enrichment call, with on-error-continue inside. The enrichment fails. What is the effect on the rest of the flow?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="orders">
  <http:listener path="/orders"/>
  <try>
    <http:request path="/enrich"/>       <!-- fails -->
    <error-handler>
      <on-error-continue>
        <set-variable variableName="enriched" value="false"/>
      </on-error-continue>
    </error-handler>
  </try>
  <db:insert .../>
  <set-payload value='{"saved": true}'/>
</flow>
options: Flow continues past the Try; vars.enriched='false'
The whole flow fails with 500
The DB insert is skipped but the set-payload runs
The Try scope retries the enrichment automatically
optionNotes: Correct — Continue inside the Try contains the failure to that scope; execution resumes AFTER the Try normally.
Wrong — that's what happens WITHOUT the Try (or with Propagate): the error would reach the flow level.
Wrong — after a handled Try, ALL subsequent processors run in order.
Wrong — Try scopes don't retry; retrying is Until Successful's job.
questions.explanation: Try + On Error Continue = 'this part may fail and that's fine': the failure is absorbed at the scope boundary and the flow proceeds — the processor-level tier of Mule error handling.

questions.q: The error object after a failed HTTP call must drive the response. Which expression returns the HTTP status code of the FAILED backend response?
options: error.errorMessage.attributes.statusCode
error.statusCode
error.errorType.status
attributes.statusCode
optionNotes: Correct — for HTTP errors, error.errorMessage carries the failed response as a Mule message: its attributes hold statusCode, headers, and its payload holds the error body.
Wrong — the error object has no direct statusCode field.
Wrong — errorType holds namespace/identifier (HTTP:NOT_FOUND), not response details.
Wrong — inside an error handler, the event's attributes are NOT the failed response's attributes; the failed response lives in error.errorMessage.
questions.explanation: error.errorMessage packages the failing operation's message: error.errorMessage.attributes.statusCode and error.errorMessage.payload (error body) — the exam loves this exact path.

questions.q: A flow calls two different services; both can raise HTTP:NOT_FOUND. The error handler must react differently depending on WHICH call failed. What is the recommended approach?
options: Map each operation's HTTP:NOT_FOUND to a custom type, then handle those types
Compare the error.description text inside a when expression to tell the two apart
It is impossible to distinguish them
Wrap the whole flow in one Try scope
optionNotes: Correct — error mapping converts source types per-operation into business-meaningful custom types, giving handlers precise targets.
Wrong — matching on message text is brittle (wording changes, localization); types are the contract.
Wrong — error mapping exists exactly for this.
Wrong — one Try around everything still yields the same HTTP:NOT_FOUND type for both calls.
questions.explanation: Error Mapping (per operation) turns generic connector errors into distinct custom types (namespace like APP:), making two identical failure modes distinguishable to handlers.

questions.q: A business rule check fails and the flow must abort with a meaningful, catchable error. Which component is designed for this?
options: Raise Error with a custom type like APP:ORDER_REJECTED and a description
Logger with level ERROR
Set Payload with an error message
A Choice route whose branch logs the reason and then ends the flow quietly
optionNotes: Correct — Raise Error throws a typed, handler-catchable Mule error with your namespace:identifier and description.
Wrong — logging records the fact but the flow continues normally.
Wrong — an error-looking payload isn't an error; downstream processing continues.
Wrong — Choice routes don't abort processing or produce error semantics.
questions.explanation: Raise Error is the explicit throw: custom type + description, catchable by on-error scopes and mappable to API error responses.

questions.q: Given the global default error handler configuration shown, which flows use 'globalHandler' when an error occurs?
questions.exhibit:
<configuration defaultErrorHandler-ref="globalHandler"/>

<flow name="a">              <!-- no error-handler element -->
<flow name="b">
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-continue type="HTTP:NOT_FOUND"/>
  </error-handler>
</flow>
options: Flow a: all errors. Flow b: none — a defined handler replaces the default
Both flows for all error types
Flow b only, as a fallback for non-matching types
Neither flow — a global handler must be explicitly referenced from within each flow
optionNotes: Correct — the configured default applies ONLY to flows with no error handling of their own; it never layers under a flow's own handler.
Wrong — flow b declared its own handler, opting out of the default completely.
Wrong — there is no fallback-chaining: an unmatched error in b propagates with built-in default behavior, not via globalHandler.
Wrong — the configuration reference is exactly how a global default is wired.
questions.explanation: The app-level default error handler is a REPLACEMENT for absent flow handlers, not a safety net beneath existing ones — the most misunderstood rule in Mule error handling.

questions.q: Which errors can NO on-error scope ever handle? (Select all that apply)
options: System errors, e.g. a connection lost while the app is idle
MULE:CRITICAL errors
HTTP:CONNECTIVITY raised during a request in a flow
Errors thrown by a Raise Error component
optionNotes: Correct — system errors involve no Mule event, so no handler context exists; Mule logs and applies reconnection.
Correct — CRITICAL errors (fatal) are not handleable by design.
Wrong — connectivity errors DURING event processing are ordinary messaging errors, fully handleable.
Wrong — Raise Error produces standard messaging errors intended to be caught.
questions.explanation: Handlers only see MESSAGING errors (event in flight). System-level failures and MULE:CRITICAL are outside their reach.

questions.q: An on-error scope must handle VALIDATION errors only when a variable indicates a retail order. Which configuration is correct?
options: type="VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL" when="#[vars.channel == 'retail']" — both conditions must hold
type="VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL OR vars.channel == 'retail'"
when="VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL"
Two nested error handlers
optionNotes: Correct — a scope can combine 'type' (error type list) AND 'when' (boolean DataWeave); it matches only when both are satisfied.
Wrong — 'type' accepts error types (comma-separated), not boolean expressions.
Wrong — 'when' takes a DataWeave BOOLEAN, not a type name.
Wrong — handlers don't nest for condition combination; type+when on one scope does it.
questions.explanation: On-error scopes match on type AND/OR when: type filters by error type; when adds an arbitrary runtime condition (evaluated against the error event).

questions.q: APIkit generated the main flow with its standard error handler. A developer's implementation flow (get:\flights:config) throws VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL from a validator, and that flow has NO error handler. What does the client receive?
options: HTTP 500 — it propagates to the main flow whose handler has no VALIDATION mapping
HTTP 400 automatically, because APIkit always classifies validation failures as client bad requests
HTTP 200 with an empty response body
The request hangs until the client eventually times out
optionNotes: Correct — unhandled errors bubble to the caller (the main flow via the router); scaffolding maps APIKIT:* types, so a VALIDATION type without custom mapping lands in the generic/ANY propagate path → 500.
Wrong — auto-mapping applies to APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST (router validation); the FLOW's own validation errors need explicit mapping/handling to become 400s.
Wrong — an unhandled error never converts to success.
Wrong — Mule always produces an error response.
questions.explanation: Router-side violations → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST → 400 automatically. IMPLEMENTATION-side validation errors are yours to map (handle VALIDATION types and set 400) or they surface as 500.

questions.q: What does this error hierarchy imply for a handler declaring type="MULE:CONNECTIVITY"?
questions.exhibit:
Error type hierarchy (excerpt):
MULE:ANY
 └─ MULE:CONNECTIVITY
     ├─ HTTP:CONNECTIVITY
     └─ DB:CONNECTIVITY
options: A parent type, so it also catches HTTP: and DB:CONNECTIVITY children
It matches only errors whose type is literally MULE:CONNECTIVITY, nothing else
It matches every Mule error
Parent types cannot be used in handlers
optionNotes: Correct — matching is hierarchical: a scope for a parent type handles all descendant types.
Wrong — the hierarchy exists precisely to allow umbrella handling; children inherit matchability.
Wrong — that's MULE:ANY, one level up.
Wrong — any type in the hierarchy is usable.
questions.explanation: Error types form a tree rooted at MULE:ANY; a handler for MULE:CONNECTIVITY catches every connector's connectivity subtype — coarse-grained retry/fallback logic in one scope.

questions.q: With NO error handler anywhere in the application, what happens when a processor in an HTTP-triggered flow fails?
options: Mule's default handler logs and propagates it; the client gets a 500
The event is silently dropped
The flow restarts from the beginning
The error is stored for later reprocessing by the runtime's built-in dead-letter queue
optionNotes: Correct — the implicit default behaves like On Error Propagate: log + propagate → listener error response.
Wrong — errors are never silent; they're always logged and answered.
Wrong — no automatic replay exists for HTTP flows.
Wrong — persistence/reprocessing requires explicit design (queues, redelivery).
questions.explanation: The built-in default error handler = log it, propagate it. For an HTTP listener that means a 500 response with the error description.

questions.q: Given this RAML, which request is VALID?
questions.exhibit:
#%RAML 1.0
title: Bookings
baseUri: http://api.acme.com/v1

/bookings:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      status:
        enum: [open, closed]
        required: true
  /{ref}:
    get:
options: GET http://api.acme.com/v1/bookings?status=open
GET http://api.acme.com/v1/bookings
GET http://api.acme.com/v1/bookings/status/open
POST http://api.acme.com/v1/bookings?status=open
optionNotes: Correct — path matches /bookings under the baseUri and the REQUIRED status query param has an allowed enum value.
Wrong — status is required: true; omitting it violates the contract (APIkit would return 400).
Wrong — status is a QUERY parameter, not a path segment; /bookings/status/open matches no declared resource.
Wrong — only get is declared on /bookings; POST would be 405 Method Not Allowed.
questions.explanation: Valid request = declared resource path + declared method + required parameters with legal values. Read the indentation: queryParameters belongs to get, /{ref} is a nested resource.

questions.q: The RAML uses a trait. What does applying it to the get method achieve?
questions.exhibit:
traits:
  pageable:
    queryParameters:
      offset:
        type: integer
        default: 0
      limit:
        type: integer
        maximum: 100

/flights:
  get:
    is: [pageable]
options: The trait's offset & limit query params merge into GET /flights
The trait creates two sub-resources /offset and /limit
The trait only documents the parameters; they are not validated
Traits can only be applied at the resource level, so this is invalid
optionNotes: Correct — 'is: [pageable]' merges the trait's method-level declarations into get: reusable pagination in one line.
Wrong — traits contribute method properties (params/headers/responses), never resources.
Wrong — trait-declared parameters are part of the contract and validated like inline ones (limit=200 → 400).
Wrong — traits apply to METHODS via is:; resourceTypes apply to resources via type:.
questions.explanation: Traits = reusable method fragments applied with is:. The merged parameters are fully enforced by APIkit. resourceTypes are the resource-level sibling (applied with type:).

questions.q: Which RAML fragment file correctly defines a reusable data type, given the header shown?
questions.exhibit:
File: dataTypes/customer.raml
#%RAML 1.0 DataType
type: object
properties:
  id: integer
  email:
    type: string
    pattern: ".+@.+"
  nickname?: string
options: It defines a Customer type where id and email are required and nickname is optional
It defines a type where all three properties are optional
It is invalid — fragments cannot declare patterns
It defines an array type
optionNotes: Correct — properties are required by default; the ? suffix (nickname?) marks optional; pattern constrains email.
Wrong — RAML properties default to required: true; only nickname? is optional.
Wrong — patterns (regex) are standard type facets, valid in fragments.
Wrong — type: object declares an object; an array would be Customer[] at the point of use.
questions.explanation: DataType fragments (#%RAML 1.0 DataType) hold one reusable type. Required-by-default and the ? optional suffix are the details exams probe.

questions.q: In the design-first lifecycle, what is the correct ORDER of activities before implementation starts?
options: Design → simulate with the mock → gather feedback → validate the contract
Implement → document → publish → design
Deploy an API proxy first, then design, then mock, and finally implement the flows
Write MUnit tests → design → publish
optionNotes: Correct — design-first iterates the CONTRACT with consumers (design/simulate/feedback/validate) until stable, then implementation begins.
Wrong — that's code-first: documentation after implementation defeats consumer collaboration.
Wrong — proxies govern deployed APIs; nothing exists to proxy yet.
Wrong — MUnit tests target an implementation, which doesn't exist yet.
questions.explanation: Design → simulate → feedback → validate is the mantra: the mocking service makes the spec testable by consumers before a single flow exists.

questions.q: Which statement about this RAML response declaration is TRUE?
questions.exhibit:
/flights:
  get:
    responses:
      200:
        body:
          application/json:
            type: Flight[]
            examples:
              output: !include examples/flights.raml
options: 200 returns a JSON Flight array; example from a NamedExample fragment
The API can only ever return exactly the contents of flights.raml
type: Flight[] means the response has a field called 'Flight'
The include makes the example mandatory in every response
optionNotes: Correct — Flight[] is an array of the Flight type; !include pulls the example from a separate fragment file (reusable, format-independent).
Wrong — examples document and power mocking; the real implementation returns live data conforming to the TYPE.
Wrong — [] is array syntax for the declared type.
Wrong — examples are documentation/mocking artifacts, not runtime constraints.
questions.explanation: responses.<code>.body.<mediaType> declares type + examples per status. Type[] = array; !include composes fragments; examples feed the console and mocking service.

questions.q: A team wants the 'Address' data type shared by five different API specs, versioned independently, without copy-paste. What is the MuleSoft-idiomatic mechanism?
options: Publish the DataType fragment to Exchange and add it to each spec as a dependency (uses:)
Paste the type into each RAML and keep them in sync manually
Define the type once in the first API and have every other API reference that API's live URL
Put the type in a shared database
optionNotes: Correct — Exchange hosts versioned reusable fragments; API Designer adds them as dependencies so specs import the same governed type.
Wrong — copy-paste drifts immediately; that's the problem fragments solve.
Wrong — RAML cannot import types from a deployed API's URL; contracts compose from fragments, not runtime endpoints.
Wrong — databases hold data, not design-time type definitions.
questions.explanation: Reusable design assets (types, traits, examples, libraries) are published to Exchange as API fragments and consumed as versioned dependencies across specs.

questions.q: What is the difference between these two declarations in a RAML spec?
questions.exhibit:
Declaration A:
/flights/{ID}:

Declaration B:
/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      ID:
options: A addresses one flight by path, B filters the list
They are interchangeable ways to pass the same value
A is invalid RAML syntax
B creates a sub-resource named ID
optionNotes: Correct — URI parameters address a specific resource (/flights/42); query parameters refine a collection request (/flights?ID=42 is unusual design, filters conceptually).
Wrong — REST semantics differ: resource identity belongs in the path; filters/options in the query string.
Wrong — {ID} nested under /flights is standard URI parameter syntax.
Wrong — queryParameters never create resources; only nested /segments do.
questions.explanation: The identity-vs-refinement rule: specific resource → URI parameter in the path; filtering/sorting/paging a collection → query parameters.

questions.q: A consumer reads the published RAML and wants to create a flight. Which request matches the contract?
questions.exhibit:
/flights:
  post:
    headers:
      client_id:
        required: true
    body:
      application/json:
        type: object
        properties:
          code: string
          price: number
options: POST /flights with header client_id and JSON body {"code": "ER38sd", "price": 400.99}
POST /flights with JSON body only — headers are always optional
PUT /flights with the same body
POST /flights with body code=ER38sd&price=400.99 as form fields
optionNotes: Correct — method, required header, media type, and body properties all match the declaration.
Wrong — client_id is required: true; omitting it violates the contract (400 from APIkit).
Wrong — only post is declared; PUT → 405.
Wrong — the declared media type is application/json, not form-encoded.
questions.explanation: Formulating requests from RAML = method + path + required headers/params + body of the declared media type and shape.

questions.q: What happens when an API specification is published to Exchange? (Select all that apply)
options: An interactive API console/portal is generated from the spec
The mocking service can serve example-based responses
Other developers can discover it and request access
A Mule implementation is automatically deployed
optionNotes: Correct — Exchange renders live documentation from the RAML/OAS.
Correct — the mocking service exposes a callable simulation using the spec's examples.
Correct — discovery and access requests (client apps, contracts) are Exchange's core purpose.
Wrong — publishing a SPEC deploys nothing; implementation and deployment are separate steps.
questions.explanation: Publishing a spec creates the consumption experience (docs, console, mock, discovery) — the implementation lifecycle is entirely separate.

questions.q: The spec declares a baseUri with a version placeholder. Which statements are TRUE?
questions.exhibit:
#%RAML 1.0
title: Orders
version: v2
baseUri: http://api.acme.com/{version}

/orders:
  get:
options: Requests resolve to /v2/orders; {version} comes from the version property
The client must send the API version in a custom request header on every call
{version} is a queryParameter
RAML forbids placeholders in baseUri
optionNotes: Correct — {version} is a reserved baseUri parameter automatically bound to the spec's version value.
Wrong — the version rides in the URL path here, not a header.
Wrong — it's a baseUri (path) parameter, not a query parameter.
Wrong — baseUri placeholders (incl. custom baseUriParameters) are standard RAML.
questions.explanation: The reserved {version} placeholder in baseUri binds to the RAML's version: property — the common URL-versioning pattern (…/v2/orders).

questions.q: Why would an API designer choose a NamedExample fragment (!include of an example file) instead of typing examples inline?
options: One example reused across methods and specs, maintained in one place
Inline examples are not allowed in RAML 1.0
NamedExample fragments are validated by the platform while inline examples are not checked
It makes the API respond faster
optionNotes: Correct — externalized examples are reusable, single-source-of-truth artifacts (and publishable to Exchange as fragments).
Wrong — inline examples are perfectly legal; externalizing is a reuse choice.
Wrong — both are validated against the declared type.
Wrong — examples are design-time; runtime performance is unaffected.
questions.explanation: Fragments (DataType, NamedExample, Trait…) exist for reuse and maintainability — one example file feeding many methods beats duplicated inline blobs.

questions.q: APIkit validates a request against this RAML. Which request triggers a 400 response?
questions.exhibit:
/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      airline:
        enum: [united, delta, american]
        required: false
      maxPrice:
        type: integer
        required: false
options: GET /flights?maxPrice=cheap
GET /flights
GET /flights?airline=delta
GET /flights?airline=delta&maxPrice=500
optionNotes: Correct — maxPrice is declared integer; 'cheap' fails type validation → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST → 400.
Wrong — both parameters are optional; a bare GET is valid.
Wrong — delta is in the enum; valid.
Wrong — both values satisfy their declarations; valid.
questions.explanation: Optional parameters may be omitted, but when PRESENT they must satisfy their type/enum. Type violations are router-level 400s.

questions.q: A Database SELECT is configured as shown. What is wrong with it, and what is the fix?
questions.exhibit:
<db:select config-ref="dbConfig">
  <db:sql>
    SELECT * FROM flights
    WHERE toAirport = '#[attributes.queryParams.dest]'
  </db:sql>
</db:select>
options: It concatenates user input into SQL (injection); use an input parameter :dest instead
SELECT * is forbidden by the Mule Database connector and must list columns explicitly
The db:sql element cannot contain WHERE clauses
Nothing is wrong
optionNotes: Correct — expression-interpolated SQL is injectable (dest = "' OR '1'='1"); :parameters with the input-parameters map fix typing, escaping, and security.
Wrong — SELECT * is legal (though explicit columns are nicer).
Wrong — arbitrary SQL is supported; the problem is HOW the value is injected.
Wrong — this is the canonical SQL injection anti-pattern.
questions.explanation: Never build SQL by embedding expressions in the string. Input parameters (:name + DataWeave map) are the Database connector's parameterized-query mechanism.

questions.q: What does the payload look like immediately after this Database operation returns two rows?
questions.exhibit:
<db:select config-ref="dbConfig">
  <db:sql>SELECT code, price FROM flights WHERE dest = :d</db:sql>
  <db:input-parameters>#[{d: 'SFO'}]</db:input-parameters>
</db:select>
options: An array of objects: [{code: ..., price: ...}, {code: ..., price: ...}]
A JSON string
A single object with arrays per column
A java.sql.ResultSet requiring manual iteration
optionNotes: Correct — Select returns an array (List) of row objects (Maps), one per row, keyed by column name.
Wrong — it's Java structure data; a Transform can output JSON from it.
Wrong — rows are elements, not parallel column arrays.
Wrong — the connector materializes/streams rows as Mule data, not raw JDBC objects.
questions.explanation: db:select → array of row objects (streamed). Transforms usually follow to shape it; the array-of-objects assumption underlies most DataWeave exercises.

questions.q: An HTTP Request operation is configured with default settings and receives a 404 from the backend. What happens?
options: The operation throws HTTP:NOT_FOUND — by default only 2xx (and 3xx per config) count as success
The 404 response becomes the payload and the flow continues normally
The request is retried three times automatically
The flow returns 404 to its own client automatically
optionNotes: Correct — non-success status codes raise typed HTTP errors (HTTP:NOT_FOUND, HTTP:INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR...).
Wrong — that requires widening the operation's success status codes setting (e.g., '200..404').
Wrong — status-code failures aren't retried by default (reconnection strategies address CONNECTION failures).
Wrong — what the flow's client receives depends on the flow's error handling, not automatic forwarding.
questions.explanation: The requester validates response status: outside the success range → typed error. The 'success status codes' setting (e.g. 200..404) opts into treating specific errors as normal responses.

questions.q: A flow consumes a SOAP service. Where does the developer see the exact XML structure the Consume operation expects, and how is the request built?
options: A Transform before the Consume operation shows the WSDL-derived SOAP body to map
The developer must read the WSDL by hand and write the entire SOAP envelope as a string
The Consume operation accepts JSON and converts it
SOAP services cannot be called from Mule 4
optionNotes: Correct — WSC exposes DataSense metadata per operation; the Transform before Consume presents the SOAP body structure (namespaces included) as the mapping target.
Wrong — the connector builds the envelope; the developer maps only the BODY, guided by metadata.
Wrong — the payload must be the XML body for the operation, produced by the preceding Transform.
Wrong — the Web Service Consumer exists precisely for SOAP.
questions.explanation: WSC pattern: configure with the WSDL → pick the operation → Transform Message immediately before Consume shows the expected XML body via metadata → map to it.

questions.q: An On New or Updated File listener keeps reprocessing the same file every poll. The exhibit shows its configuration. What is missing?
questions.exhibit:
<file:listener directory="/data/in" matcher="csvOnly">
  <scheduling-strategy>
    <fixed-frequency frequency="10" timeUnit="SECONDS"/>
  </scheduling-strategy>
</file:listener>
options: Post-process the file: move, rename, or delete it after processing
A bigger polling frequency
A Transform Message placed inside the listener to mark files as processed
The matcher must be removed
optionNotes: Correct — without post-processing (move/rename/delete) or a watermark, the file still matches on every poll and is picked up again.
Wrong — slower polling only reprocesses less often; the duplicate processing remains.
Wrong — listeners don't contain processors; the problem is source configuration.
Wrong — the matcher just filters WHICH files match; it doesn't prevent re-matching.
questions.explanation: File listener hygiene: always configure post-processing (moveToDirectory + rename, or auto-delete) — or watermarking on timestamps — so processed files stop matching.

questions.q: Which JMS operation pattern implements REQUEST-REPLY over queues (send a message and wait for the answer)?
options: Publish-Consume — publishes to a queue and waits for the response on a reply queue
Publish followed by an unrelated Consume somewhere else
On New Message
Two Publish operations
optionNotes: Correct — Publish-Consume is the built-in request-reply operation: publish + block for the correlated reply.
Wrong — uncorrelated operations don't implement request-reply semantics reliably.
Wrong — On New Message is a listener SOURCE, not a request-reply client.
Wrong — two publishes send two messages; nothing waits for a reply.
questions.explanation: JMS operations: Publish (fire), Consume (pull one), Publish-Consume (request-reply with correlation), On New Message (listener source).

questions.q: A nightly integration must pick up orders inserted into a database table by another system. Which source is designed for this?
options: The Database connector's On Table Row listener (polls using a watermark column)
An HTTP Listener
A JMS On New Message listener subscribed to the database's change-notification topic
The File listener
optionNotes: Correct — On Table Row polls the table and uses a watermark column (id/timestamp) to emit only new rows.
Wrong — nothing calls the app; the data appears in a TABLE.
Wrong — there is no queue in this scenario.
Wrong — the data is rows, not files.
questions.explanation: Match source to signal: table inserts → On Table Row (with watermark); files → File listener; messages → JMS/MQ listener; requests → HTTP Listener.

questions.q: The exhibit shows an HTTP Request. Which statements about its behavior are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
questions.exhibit:
<http:request method="POST" config-ref="apiConfig" path="/orders/{oid}/items">
  <http:uri-params>#[{oid: vars.orderId}]</http:uri-params>
  <http:query-params>#[{expand: 'lines'}]</http:query-params>
</http:request>
options: {oid} in the path is replaced by vars.orderId at runtime
The request URL ends with ?expand=lines
The current payload is sent as the POST body by default
uri-params requires the RAML to declare oid
optionNotes: Correct — http:uri-params fills path template placeholders.
Correct — query-params serialize onto the query string.
Correct — the requester sends the message payload as the body unless a body expression overrides it.
Wrong — the REQUEST side is independent of any RAML; templates are purely the client's URL construction.
questions.explanation: HTTP Request anatomy: path templates ({x} + uri-params map), query-params map, body = payload by default, headers configurable — all DataWeave-driven.

questions.q: Why choose a REST Connector from Exchange (REST Connect) over a raw HTTP Request to consume a published API?
options: One typed operation per method with DataSense metadata, not hand-built request URLs
It is the only supported way to call secured HTTPS endpoints from within a Mule application
It bypasses the API's policies
It removes the need for authentication
optionNotes: Correct — the generated connector encodes the contract: operations, parameters, and metadata for mapping — less error-prone than manual paths.
Wrong — HTTP Request handles HTTPS fully (TLS context).
Wrong — policies apply at the gateway regardless of client type.
Wrong — configured authentication is still required; the connector just structures it.
questions.explanation: REST Connect turns a published spec into a typed connector — contract-aware consumption with metadata, versus hand-maintained HTTP Request configs.

questions.q: A flow writes a file with the File connector. The exhibit shows the config. What happens if /data/out/report.csv already exists?
questions.exhibit: <file:write path="/data/out/report.csv" mode="CREATE_NEW"/>
options: The operation fails with FILE:FILE_ALREADY_EXISTS
The file is silently overwritten
The content is appended
A numbered copy report(1).csv is created
optionNotes: Correct — CREATE_NEW writes only when the target doesn't exist; an existing file raises the typed error.
Wrong — silent overwrite is mode=OVERWRITE (the default).
Wrong — appending is mode=APPEND.
Wrong — the connector never invents file names.
questions.explanation: file:write modes: OVERWRITE (default), APPEND, CREATE_NEW (fail if exists) — pick by idempotency/dedup requirements.

questions.q: Which consequence follows from JMS queues DECOUPLING producer and consumer?
options: The producer can keep publishing while the consumer is down; messages wait in the queue
The producer receives the consumer's return value synchronously
Both applications must be deployed together
Messages are lost if not consumed within seconds
optionNotes: Correct — temporal decoupling is the core benefit: the broker buffers until consumers return.
Wrong — queue messaging is asynchronous; there is no return value (request-reply needs an explicit reply queue).
Wrong — independence of deployment/availability is the point.
Wrong — persistent messages wait according to broker retention, not seconds.
questions.explanation: Messaging decouples in time and availability: producers and consumers run, fail, and scale independently, with the broker buffering in between.

questions.q: A Database config needs the MySQL driver. In Studio the config panel shows 'Configure via Maven dependency'. What does completing this do to the project?
options: Adds the driver's groupId/artifactId/version to pom.xml so it loads at runtime
Downloads a .jar into src/main/resources
Nothing — drivers ship with the runtime
Adds only a <sharedLibraries> entry to the pom, without the Maven dependency itself
optionNotes: Correct — the driver is declared in the POM (and Studio also registers it as a shared library so the connector's classloader can see it).
Wrong — dependencies are managed by Maven, not loose JARs in resources.
Wrong — Mule ships no vendor JDBC drivers; each app declares its own.
Wrong — the sharedLibraries entry references a dependency that must ALSO exist.
