questions.explanation: JDBC drivers are Maven dependencies declared in pom.xml (plus shared-library visibility for the connector) — Studio's Database config wizard automates both parts.

questions.q: What is the payload after this Scatter-Gather completes successfully?
questions.exhibit:
<scatter-gather>
  <route>   <!-- route 0: returns ["A","B"] -->
  <route>   <!-- route 1: returns ["C"] -->
</scatter-gather>
<!-- followed by: -->
<ee:transform>
  %dw 2.0
  output application/json
  ---
  flatten(payload pluck $.payload)
</ee:transform>
options: After the transform: ["A", "B", "C"]
After the transform: [["A","B"], ["C"]]
After the transform: {"0": ["A","B"], "1": ["C"]}
The transform fails — Scatter-Gather output is an array
optionNotes: Correct — S-G produces {"0": {attributes, payload: [A,B]}, "1": {..., payload: [C]}}; pluck extracts the payloads → [[A,B],[C]]; flatten merges → [A,B,C].
Wrong — that's the intermediate result BEFORE flatten.
Wrong — that's (approximately) the raw router output BEFORE the transform (values are full messages, not just payloads).
Wrong — the router output is an OBJECT keyed by route index, which pluck handles.
questions.explanation: The canonical aggregation: Scatter-Gather → object of route messages → pluck $.payload → array of payloads → flatten. Each intermediate shape is a distractor.

questions.q: One route of a Scatter-Gather fails with HTTP:CONNECTIVITY while the other two succeed. Default configuration — what error reaches the flow's handler?
options: MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING, aggregating the per-route failure details
HTTP:CONNECTIVITY directly
No error — failed routes are ignored
MULE:ANY with no details
optionNotes: Correct — the router wraps route failures in COMPOSITE_ROUTING; error.errorMessage/childErrors expose which route failed and why.
Wrong — the original type is inside the composite, not thrown directly.
Wrong — by default any route failure fails the router (tolerating failures needs Try scopes inside routes).
Wrong — the composite error carries full per-route detail.
questions.explanation: Scatter-Gather runs all routes; any failure surfaces as MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING after all complete. Per-route tolerance = Try + On Error Continue INSIDE the routes.

questions.q: Which requests take route 1 in this Choice router?
questions.exhibit:
<choice>
  <when expression="#[payload.total > 1000]">        <!-- route 1 -->
  <when expression="#[payload.total > 100]">         <!-- route 2 -->
  <otherwise>                                        <!-- route 3 -->
</choice>
options: Only total > 1000 — top-down, first true condition wins
All payloads with total > 100, because both conditions overlap
None — overlapping conditions are a configuration error
Route 1 and route 2 both execute for total = 2000
optionNotes: Correct — 2000 matches route 1 first and stops there; 500 skips route 1, matches route 2.
Wrong — a payload of 2000 never reaches route 2; first match wins.
Wrong — overlap is legal; ordering resolves it (most specific first is the design guideline).
Wrong — exactly ONE route executes in a Choice.
questions.explanation: Choice = if/else-if/else: ordered evaluation, single route execution. Overlapping ranges are resolved purely by declaration order.

questions.q: A flow must try a primary payment provider and, only if it errors, try the backup provider. Which router is purpose-built for this?
options: First Successful — tries routes in order until one completes without error
Scatter-Gather with two routes
Round Robin
Choice with a when condition on vars.attempt that counts and routes the retries
optionNotes: Correct — First Successful is the failover router: route 2 runs only when route 1 fails.
Wrong — S-G runs BOTH concurrently: the backup would be charged too.
Wrong — Round Robin alternates per EVENT (load distribution), regardless of errors.
Wrong — Choice picks by condition, not by failure of a previous attempt.
questions.explanation: Failover chains = First Successful. Parallel fan-out = Scatter-Gather. Alternating distribution = Round Robin. Conditional = Choice. Matching router to intent is the whole game.

questions.q: Each incoming event must be distributed to exactly ONE of three routes, rotating evenly (event 1 → route 1, event 2 → route 2, ...). Which router?
options: Round Robin
Scatter-Gather
Choice
First Successful
optionNotes: Correct — Round Robin cycles through its routes, one per event.
Wrong — Scatter-Gather sends EVERY event to ALL routes.
Wrong — Choice picks by condition, not rotation.
Wrong — First Successful is failover, not distribution.
questions.explanation: Round Robin = simple load distribution across routes, one route per event in rotation.

questions.q: The validation shown runs against payload {"email": "nope"}. What EXACTLY happens?
questions.exhibit:
<validation:is-email email="#[payload.email]" message="Bad address supplied"/>
<logger message="validated ok"/>
options: VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL is thrown; the logger does not run
The validator returns false and the logger logs 'validated ok'
The payload is replaced with a validation report
The flow returns 400 automatically
optionNotes: Correct — validators are fail-fast throwers; the custom message becomes error.description; execution jumps to error handling.
Wrong — validators never return booleans into the flow.
Wrong — on success the event passes through UNTOUCHED; on failure an error is raised.
Wrong — the HTTP status depends on how the flow's error handling maps the error (400 requires explicit handling).
questions.explanation: Validation module semantics: pass → no-op; fail → typed VALIDATION error with your message. HTTP mapping is the handler's job.

questions.q: Three independent backends must be queried and ALL results combined; total latency must be minimized. The calls take 300 ms, 500 ms, 400 ms. Which construct and expected duration?
options: Scatter-Gather; ≈500 ms (the slowest route)
Three sequential HTTP Requests; ≈500 ms
Scatter-Gather; ≈1200 ms (sum of routes)
Async scope; ≈0 ms and results available immediately
optionNotes: Correct — concurrent routes overlap; the router waits for all, so total ≈ max(300,500,400).
Wrong — sequential calls ADD up: ≈1200 ms.
Wrong — routes run concurrently, not sequentially.
Wrong — Async doesn't return results to the flow at all.
questions.explanation: Parallel fan-out cuts aggregate latency to the slowest branch. Async is fire-and-forget (no results); sequential requests sum their latencies.

questions.q: Inside a Scatter-Gather, each route sets vars.routeResult. After the router, what is the state of that variable in the main flow?
options: Each route ran on its own event copy; those vars aren't merged into the flow's vars
vars.routeResult holds the LAST route's value
vars.routeResult is an array of all values
Setting variables inside a Scatter-Gather route raises a MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING error
optionNotes: Correct — routes receive copies; their variable changes live in each route's resulting message (accessible via the aggregate object), not in the continuing event's vars.
Wrong — no last-write-wins merge occurs for route variables.
Wrong — no automatic array merge exists; you aggregate explicitly from the router output.
Wrong — it's legal; the values are just scoped to the copies.
questions.explanation: Scatter-Gather copies the event per route (immutability in action). Route-local changes are collected in the aggregate result object rather than merged into flow variables.

questions.q: Which Validation component composes several checks so that ALL must pass?
options: The All validator grouping several validators
The Any validator
A Choice router
Validators cannot be combined together into one component
optionNotes: Correct — All aggregates validators; it fails if ANY inner validation fails (reporting the failures).
Wrong — Any passes when AT LEAST ONE inner validation passes.
Wrong — Choice routes; it doesn't aggregate validation semantics.
Wrong — All/Any exist precisely for composition.
questions.explanation: validation:all = logical AND of validators; validation:any = logical OR. Both raise a single composite validation error on failure.

questions.q: A Choice router has three when routes and NO otherwise. An event matches none of the conditions. What happens?
options: The event continues to the processor AFTER the Choice, unchanged
A MULE:ROUTING error is thrown
The event is dropped
The first route executes as a fallback
optionNotes: Correct — with no otherwise and no match, the Choice is a no-op and processing resumes after it.
Wrong — Mule 4's Choice doesn't error on no-match (unlike some Mule 3 configurations).
Wrong — events are never silently dropped by a Choice.
Wrong — routes execute only when their condition is true.
questions.explanation: No match + no otherwise = pass-through. Add an otherwise when a default action (or explicit error) is required.

questions.q: Trace this flow. The payload arriving at the Logger AFTER the batch job is what?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="sync">
  <scheduler .../>
  <db:select .../>              <!-- returns 5,000 rows -->
  <batch:job jobName="syncJob">
    <batch:process-records>
      <batch:step name="upsert"> ... </batch:step>
    </batch:process-records>
    <batch:on-complete> ... </batch:on-complete>
  </batch:job>
  <logger message="#[payload]"/>   <!-- what prints? -->
</flow>
options: The original payload; the flow continues while the batch runs asynchronously
The BatchJobResult object holding total, successful, and failed record counts
The last record processed
null — the batch job consumes the payload
optionNotes: Correct — Load & Dispatch queues the records and returns control; the triggering flow proceeds with the ORIGINAL payload while processing happens in the background.
Wrong — BatchJobResult is the ON COMPLETE phase's payload, inside the job, not the flow's.
Wrong — records live inside the job's queues; they don't leak into the flow.
Wrong — the payload is preserved for the continuing flow.
questions.explanation: batch:job is asynchronous: the caller keeps its payload and moves on immediately. Results/statistics are visible only in On Complete — one of the most-tested batch facts.

questions.q: Which input types can the Batch Job component split into records WITHOUT a preceding transform?
options: Java Iterables/Iterators/Arrays, and JSON or XML array-like payloads
Only Java Lists
Only CSV files
Any payload, including single objects — everything becomes one record each
optionNotes: Correct — the job splits collection-like inputs (Java collections/iterators/arrays; JSON and XML payloads with repeating structure).
Wrong — Lists work, but so do the other collection-like forms.
Wrong — CSV must first be READ into a structured collection (e.g., by a reader/transform).
Wrong — non-collection payloads require transformation into a splittable structure first.
questions.explanation: Batch's Load & Dispatch splits Iterables/Iterators/Arrays and JSON/XML collections. Anything else is transformed first — commonly the output of a DB select or a parsed file.

questions.q: A batch job has maxFailedRecords="-1" and three steps; step 3 is configured as shown. Record R fails in step 1. What is R's journey?
questions.exhibit:
<batch:step name="step3" acceptPolicy="ONLY_FAILURES">
  <ee:transform> ... build error report entry ... </ee:transform>
</batch:step>
options: R skips step 2 but enters step 3 (builds the error report)
R is discarded immediately after failing step 1
R retries step 1 until it succeeds
R stops the whole job
optionNotes: Correct — failed records skip subsequent NO_FAILURES steps but match an ONLY_FAILURES step — the standard per-record error-reporting pattern.
Wrong — with maxFailedRecords=-1, failures are tolerated and tracked, not discarded from the job.
Wrong — batch steps don't retry records automatically.
Wrong — -1 means unlimited failed records; the job continues.
questions.explanation: Accept policies route records by status: NO_FAILURES (default), ONLY_FAILURES (error-handling steps), ALL. maxFailedRecords=-1 keeps the job alive regardless of failures.

questions.q: Why place a Batch Aggregator (size=200) inside a batch step that upserts to Salesforce?
questions.exhibit:
<batch:step name="upsert">
  <batch:aggregator size="200">
    <salesforce:upsert .../>   <!-- receives an ARRAY of 200 records -->
  </batch:aggregator>
</batch:step>
options: Collect records into groups of 200 for one bulk call per group
To deliberately slow down processing so downstream rate limits aren't exceeded
To guarantee record ordering
To retry failed records
optionNotes: Correct — aggregators batch records for BULK operations: 5,000 records become 25 upsert calls instead of 5,000.
Wrong — throughput goes UP, not down; rate-limit protection is a side benefit but not the mechanism's purpose.
Wrong — batch processing is parallel; aggregation doesn't impose global order.
Wrong — retry/error handling is separate (accept policies, connector retries).
questions.explanation: batch:aggregator (fixed size or streaming) turns record-at-a-time steps into array-at-a-time calls — the pattern for bulk-capable targets like Salesforce or DB bulk inserts.

questions.q: A variable is set in batch step 1 for record R (vars.score = 88). Which statements are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: Step 2 sees vars.score = 88 when processing the SAME record R
Other records do NOT see R's value — record variables are per record
The On Complete phase can read vars.score
Variables cannot be set inside batch steps
optionNotes: Correct — record variables propagate ACROSS STEPS for the same record.
Correct — each record carries its own variable set (records are independent events).
Wrong — Process-phase variables do NOT reach On Complete; it only receives the BatchJobResult.
Wrong — setting vars per record inside steps is standard practice (e.g., for conditional accept expressions).
questions.explanation: Record vars: per-record, cross-step, gone by On Complete. They drive per-record routing (accept expressions) and enrichment between steps.

questions.q: Compare For Each and Parallel For Each for a collection of 100 API calls. Which statements are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: For Each processes sequentially and returns the ORIGINAL payload afterwards
Parallel For Each processes concurrently and returns an ARRAY of each iteration's result
Payload changes inside For Each persist after the scope
Parallel For Each requires an Enterprise runtime cluster
optionNotes: Correct — sequential, one at a time, original payload restored after the scope.
Correct — concurrent execution; the results are aggregated into an array (order preserved by position).
Wrong — For Each discards payload modifications (vars persist, payload doesn't).
Wrong — Parallel For Each is a standard scope, no cluster required.
questions.explanation: For Each: sequential, payload restored, vars persist. Parallel For Each: concurrent, results array, composite error on failures. Batch: the heavy-duty asynchronous option.

questions.q: This Scheduler must run at 02:30 every weekday. Which cron expression is correct?
questions.exhibit:
<scheduler>
  <scheduling-strategy>
    <cron expression="?"/>
  </scheduling-strategy>
</scheduler>
options: 0 30 2 ? * MON-FRI
2:30 MON-FRI
optionNotes: Correct — Quartz cron: seconds(0) minutes(30) hours(2) day-of-month(?) month(*) day-of-week(MON-FRI).
Wrong — field order is wrong; Quartz starts with SECONDS.
Wrong — 0/30 in the minutes field means 'every 30 minutes during hour 2', not once at 02:30.
Wrong — clock-style text is not cron syntax.
questions.explanation: Mule schedulers use Quartz cron with SIX+ fields starting at seconds: '0 30 2 ? * MON-FRI' = 02:30:00 Monday through Friday.

questions.q: This flow implements a manual watermark. Which step is MISSING for it to work across app restarts?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="delta-sync">
  <scheduler .../>
  <os:retrieve key="lastSync" objectStore="syncStore">
    <os:default-value>"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"</os:default-value>
  </os:retrieve>
  <set-variable variableName="since" value="#[payload]"/>
  <http:request path="/changes">
    <http:query-params>#[{since: vars.since}]</http:query-params>
  </http:request>
  <!-- ... process the changes ... -->
</flow>
options: An os:store writing the new max timestamp to 'lastSync' after success
A bigger default value
An os:remove before each run
Nothing — the os:retrieve alone is enough to maintain the watermark automatically
optionNotes: Correct — without storing the new high-water mark, every run re-reads from the same old value; the store-back after success completes the cycle.
Wrong — the default only seeds the FIRST run.
Wrong — removing the key would reset the sync every run — the opposite of a watermark.
Wrong — retrieve reads; nothing shown ever advances the mark.
questions.explanation: Manual watermark = retrieve (with default) → query since → process → STORE the new maximum. Store only after success, or failures skip records.

questions.q: The On Table Row listener is configured with watermark column 'id' and ID column 'id'. What do these settings achieve?
options: The watermark tracks the highest id seen, so only newer rows are emitted each poll
They enable deleting rows after processing
They create an index on the table
They are decorative; without them the source would still re-read the entire table each poll
optionNotes: Correct — watermark = automatic 'greater than last seen' filtering (persisted); ID column = duplicate-emission protection within the polling window.
Wrong — the listener never deletes source rows.
Wrong — Mule doesn't alter database schemas.
Wrong — with a watermark, each poll fetches only NEW rows.
questions.explanation: On Table Row's automatic watermarking persists the max watermark-column value between polls (and restarts); the ID column deduplicates in-flight rows.

questions.q: Which Object Store operation both reads a stored value AND provides a fallback for the first execution?
options: os:retrieve with a default-value
os:store with overwrite=false
os:contains followed by os:remove
os:clear
optionNotes: Correct — retrieve returns the stored value, or the configured default when the key doesn't exist yet.
Wrong — store WRITES; it doesn't read.
Wrong — contains only answers true/false; remove deletes.
Wrong — clear empties the whole store.
questions.explanation: os:retrieve + default-value is the idiomatic 'read or initialize' — the first piece of every manual watermark.

questions.q: 1 million records arrive nightly. Requirements: survive an app crash mid-run; a single bad record must not stop the rest; report success/failure counts at the end. Map each requirement to the batch feature that satisfies it.
options: Persistent queues for crash survival; maxFailedRecords=-1 for tolerance; On Complete report
Crash survival = For Each; tolerance = Try scope; report = Logger
Crash survival = maxFailedRecords; tolerance = persistent queues; report = accept policies filter
Batch cannot satisfy these requirements
optionNotes: Correct — each requirement maps to a distinct batch capability: queued records resume after restart; failure tolerance is configured; statistics arrive in On Complete.
Wrong — For Each is in-memory and sequential: a crash loses everything.
Wrong — the features are shuffled into the wrong requirements.
Wrong — this is precisely the batch component's designed use case.
questions.explanation: Batch = the reliability trifecta for large datasets: persistent queues (restart resilience), maxFailedRecords/accept policies (record-level tolerance), BatchJobResult (reporting).

questions.q: A For Each is configured with a collection expression and batch size. What does this configuration do?
questions.exhibit:
<foreach collection="#[payload.orders]" batchSize="50">
  <db:insert .../>
</foreach>
options: Iterates over payload.orders (not the root), giving the insert 50-element chunks
Processes the first 50 orders only
Runs 50 parallel threads
The batchSize attribute converts the For Each scope into a Batch Job automatically
optionNotes: Correct — collection selects WHAT to iterate; batchSize groups elements so each iteration's payload is a chunk (array) of up to 50.
Wrong — all elements are processed; they're just grouped.
Wrong — For Each remains sequential; parallelism needs Parallel For Each.
Wrong — the scopes are unrelated; batchSize is just chunking.
questions.explanation: For Each options: collection (any array expression, default #[payload]) and batchSize (chunked iterations — handy for bulk inserts without a full Batch Job).

questions.q: Read the log excerpt. What is the root cause?
questions.exhibit:
ERROR 2026-07-01 09:14:22,101 [[MuleRuntime].uber.07: [orders-api].post:\orders:api-config]
******************************
Message               : Cannot coerce String (ABC) to Number
Error type            : MULE:EXPRESSION
Element               : post:\orders:api-config/processors/1 @ orders-api
  (Transform Message)
******************************
options: The Transform in POST /orders failed converting 'ABC' to a Number
The HTTP Listener rejected the request
The target database column is defined with the wrong data type for the value
A missing Maven dependency
optionNotes: Correct — Error type MULE:EXPRESSION + the coercion message + Element pointing at processors/1 (the Transform) identify a DataWeave coercion failure.
Wrong — the failing Element is the Transform, not the listener; the request was routed fine.
Wrong — no DB element appears in the trace.
Wrong — dependency problems fail at BUILD/startup, not as EXPRESSION errors mid-flow.
questions.explanation: Log anatomy: Message (what) + Error type (classification) + Element (which processor in which flow). Read those three lines before anything else.

questions.q: An application fails to start with the log shown. What is the fix?
options: Supply the missing property in the env file or as a -D property
Reinstall the Mule runtime
Add the property directly inside the flow XML as a hardcoded variable value
Delete the ${db.password} reference
optionNotes: Correct — an unresolved ${placeholder} at startup means no source (file, -D, deployment property) defines it for this environment.
Wrong — this is app configuration, not a runtime installation issue.
Wrong — placeholders resolve from configuration sources, not flow variables.
Wrong — the connector needs the password; removing the reference breaks it differently.
questions.explanation: 'Couldn't find configuration property value for key X' = the classic missing-environment-property startup failure — check the ${env}.yaml file loaded and deployment properties.

questions.q: In the Studio debugger, execution is paused on a breakpoint at a Choice router. The developer wants to check what #[payload.total > 1000] would evaluate to. What is the fastest way?
options: Type it into the debugger's expression evaluator against the paused event
Add a Logger, redeploy, and re-run
Read the raw bytes in the console
DataWeave expressions cannot be evaluated while execution is paused on a breakpoint
optionNotes: Correct — the Mule Debugger's expression evaluator runs arbitrary DataWeave against the current (paused) event.
Wrong — that works but costs a redeploy cycle; the evaluator answers instantly.
Wrong — raw console output doesn't evaluate expressions.
Wrong — evaluating expressions is a core debugger feature.
questions.explanation: The debugger's expression evaluator is the fast feedback loop: try selectors and conditions against the live event before writing them into components.

questions.q: A Logger prints: 'org.mule.runtime.core.internal.streaming...ManagedCursorStreamProvider@3f2a'. Why, and what prints the actual content?
options: The payload is a stream; log #[payload as String] to see its content (this consumes it)
The payload is encrypted
The Logger's level is set too low, so it prints the object's class name instead of the content
The payload is null
optionNotes: Correct — streamed payloads print as cursor providers; converting to String materializes the content (with the memory/consumption caveat).
Wrong — encryption isn't involved; it's lazy streaming.
Wrong — level affects whether the line prints, not what the object renders as.
Wrong — null prints as 'null', not a class name.
questions.explanation: Cursor/stream objects log as class names. #[payload as String] (or a prior transform) shows content — but materializes the stream; think before doing it with large payloads.

questions.q: The app fails to start with: 'Address already in use: bind 0.0.0.0:8081'. What does it mean and what are valid remedies? (Select all that apply)
options: Another process (often another Mule app in the same Studio) already listens on port 8081
Stop the other application, or change this app's http.port property
The HTTP Listener configuration is referencing a missing TLS context
Reinstalling Studio is required
optionNotes: Correct — the OS allows one listener per port; running two default-port apps collides.
Correct — free the port or parameterize/change it (${http.port}).
Wrong — TLS misconfiguration produces different errors, not a bind conflict.
Wrong — nothing is broken in Studio; it's a port conflict.
questions.explanation: Bind errors = port conflicts. In Studio it's usually a previous run still deployed; in shared runtimes, parameterize ports per app (or use a domain's shared listener).

questions.q: A breakpoint on the third processor never triggers, but the flow's error handler logs an error each request. What is the MOST likely explanation?
options: An earlier processor (1 or 2) throws before execution reaches the breakpoint
Breakpoints only take effect on Transform Message components, not on Choice routers
The debugger port is wrong
The flow is not deployed
optionNotes: Correct — the handler firing proves the flow RUNS; not reaching processor 3 means the failure happens earlier. Move the breakpoint upstream (or break on exception).
Wrong — breakpoints attach to any processor.
Wrong — a wrong debug port would prevent ALL debugging, including seeing the app at all in the debugger.
Wrong — the error handler logging shows the flow executes.
questions.explanation: Debugging logic: handler activity + unreached breakpoint = failure upstream of the breakpoint. Step from the source, or use exception breakpoints to stop where the error is raised.

questions.q: Where does a Mule application's Log4j 2 configuration live, and what does the Logger component's 'category' feed into?
options: src/main/resources/log4j2.xml; categories match logger names to set level/appender
mule-artifact.json, where logger category settings are ignored by the runtime at startup
The Runtime Manager UI only
pom.xml
optionNotes: Correct — each app ships its log4j2.xml; Logger categories (e.g., com.acme.orders) are matched by <Logger name=...> rules for filtering and routing.
Wrong — mule-artifact.json is the app descriptor (minMuleVersion, secureProperties).
Wrong — platform log VIEWERS read output; configuration is in the app.
Wrong — pom.xml is the build, not logging config.
questions.explanation: log4j2.xml (in resources) defines appenders and per-category levels; meaningful Logger categories let operators tune verbosity without touching flows.

questions.q: Maven fails with 'Could not resolve dependencies ... mule-http-connector'. Which checks are appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: Verify the dependency's version exists and the coordinates are correct
Check settings.xml credentials/repository access (corporate mirror or Exchange)
Force a refresh: mvn clean install -U (or Studio's Update project dependencies)
Delete the project and start over
optionNotes: Correct — typo'd versions/coordinates are the first suspect.
Correct — unreachable or unauthenticated repositories cause resolution failures, especially behind mirrors.
Correct — -U retries cached failed lookups (Maven caches 'not found').
Wrong — the project isn't the problem; resolution config is.
questions.explanation: Dependency triage: coordinates/version → repository reachability/credentials (settings.xml) → -U to bust the negative cache.

questions.q: Given the two property sources shown and a deployment with -Dhttp.port=9090, what is the value of ${http.port} at runtime?
questions.exhibit:
config/dev.yaml (loaded via configuration-properties):
http:
  port: "8081"

Deployment command:
mvn ... -Denv=dev -Dhttp.port=9090
options: 9090 — -D properties override file values
8081 — files always win
Both are used on alternating requests
Deployment fails at startup due to the property conflict
optionNotes: Correct — precedence puts deployment/system properties above configuration files: 9090 wins.
Wrong — files are the DEFAULTS that higher-precedence sources override.
Wrong — properties resolve once at startup, deterministically.
Wrong — overriding is by design, not a conflict.
questions.explanation: Property precedence (high→low): deployment/system properties → environment-specific sources → packaged files. This enables build-once-deploy-many.

questions.q: How does an application select dev.yaml vs prod.yaml at deploy time with ONE packaged artifact?
questions.exhibit: <configuration-properties file="config/${env}.yaml"/>
options: The file name uses ${env}; deployment passes -Denv=dev/prod
Both files load and merge automatically
The runtime detects the environment automatically from the server hostname
Two different JARs must be built
optionNotes: Correct — the properties FILE PATH itself is a placeholder resolved from the env system/deployment property.
Wrong — only the resolved file loads; there is no automatic merge of all environment files.
Wrong — no hostname magic exists; the property must be provided.
Wrong — defeating build-once is exactly what this pattern avoids.
questions.explanation: config/${env}.yaml + -Denv=<name> is the canonical environment-selection idiom — one artifact, environment decided at deployment.

questions.q: A flow must reference the property db.host inside a DataWeave transform (not a config field). Which expression is correct?
options: p('db.host')
${db.host}
vars.db.host
properties.db.host
optionNotes: Correct — the Mule::p() function reads configuration properties from DataWeave.
Wrong — ${} placeholders work in COMPONENT FIELDS (resolved at startup), not inside DataWeave scripts.
Wrong — properties are not variables.
Wrong — there is no 'properties' binding in DataWeave.
questions.explanation: Two access paths: ${prop} in XML attribute values; p('prop') inside DataWeave expressions/scripts.

questions.q: An app has apis.xml (flows + HTTP Listener referencing 'httpConfig') and global.xml (the httpConfig listener config). What makes this work, and why structure it this way?
options: All files in src/main/mule combine into one app at deploy; globals centralize config
apis.xml must import global.xml with an <import> tag or it fails
It only works if the files are alphabetically ordered
Global elements must be redefined inside every configuration file that references them
optionNotes: Correct — the runtime merges every configuration file; cross-file references (config-ref) resolve app-wide. Dedicated global.xml is a readability/reuse convention.
Wrong — no import mechanism is needed between an app's own config files.
Wrong — file naming/order does not affect resolution.
Wrong — one definition serves the whole application.
questions.explanation: An app's XML files form ONE configuration at deploy time; global elements defined anywhere are referenceable everywhere — hence the global.xml convention.

questions.q: Which execution model differences apply when calling reusable logic via Flow Reference to a SUBFLOW vs posting to a VM queue consumed by another flow? (Select all that apply)
options: Subflow call: synchronous, same event, caller waits for the result
VM publish (one-way): asynchronous, a NEW event is created for the consuming flow
Variables cross the VM queue automatically
Both behave identically
optionNotes: Correct — Flow References are in-line synchronous invocations sharing the event.
Correct — VM decouples: the consumer gets a new event (payload transferred; attributes/vars do not follow).
Wrong — the transport boundary strips vars/attributes; only the payload (and configured properties) crosses.
Wrong — synchronicity, event identity, and error propagation all differ.
questions.explanation: Flow Reference = synchronous call semantics. VM = messaging semantics (async decoupling, new event, no vars). Choosing between them is an architecture decision, not syntax.

questions.q: Which project location holds DataWeave module files, property files, and other classpath resources?
options: src/main/resources
src/main/mule
src/test/munit
target/classes
optionNotes: Correct — resources (yaml/properties, DW libraries, schemas, examples) live in src/main/resources.
Wrong — src/main/mule holds the Mule configuration XMLs.
Wrong — that's the MUnit test suite folder.
Wrong — target/ is generated build output, never source.
questions.explanation: Project layout: src/main/mule (flows), src/main/resources (classpath resources), src/test/munit (tests), pom.xml + mule-artifact.json (build/descriptor).

questions.q: Two teams each own a Mule app deployed to the SAME customer-hosted runtime, and both need to listen on port 8443 with the same TLS certificate. What enables this?
options: A Mule domain with the shared HTTPS Listener config; both apps reference it
Both apps define listeners on 8443 — the runtime multiplexes automatically
Deploying one app inside the other
This requires CloudHub
optionNotes: Correct — domains share global configs (listener/port/TLS) across apps on one runtime; both apps bind through the shared listener.
Wrong — two independent binds on one port collide (Address already in use).
Wrong — apps don't nest.
Wrong — CloudHub doesn't even support domains; this is a customer-hosted feature.
questions.explanation: Domains = shared resources for co-located apps on customer-hosted runtimes: one port, one TLS config, many apps. Not available on CloudHub (isolated workers).

questions.q: What is the effect of marking properties as secure and referencing them as shown?
questions.exhibit:
config file:
db:
  password: "![jd8sK2n...==]"

flow config field:
password="${secure::db.password}"
options: Decrypted at runtime using a deployment key; secure:: is required
The password is stored in plain text, just tagged with a marker prefix
secure:: makes Mule hash the value
The syntax is invalid
optionNotes: Correct — ![...] wraps the encrypted value; the Secure Configuration Properties module decrypts using the runtime-provided key; secure:: is the mandatory access prefix.
Wrong — the value is ciphertext, not marked plaintext.
Wrong — decryption (reversible with the key), not hashing.
Wrong — this is exactly the secure-properties syntax.
questions.explanation: Secure properties basics (deep-dive in MCD L2): encrypt values as ![...], reference via ${secure::...}, supply the key at deploy time — never packaged.

questions.q: An API on CloudHub must keep serving requests while a worker is restarted by the platform, and must handle 3× current load next quarter. Which deployment change addresses BOTH?
options: Scale out to multiple workers so the load balancer keeps it available during restarts
Deploy a single much larger worker with additional vCores to absorb the restart window
Enable persistent queues
Move the app to another region
optionNotes: Correct — 2+ workers = surviving instances keep serving (HA) AND capacity scales with count.
Wrong — a single worker of any size is still a single point of failure during restarts.
Wrong — persistent queues protect QUEUED MESSAGES, not HTTP availability.
Wrong — region choice affects latency/residency, not HA within the deployment.
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling on CloudHub answers both availability (workers fail independently) and throughput (linear capacity for stateless apps).

questions.q: The exhibit shows an autodiscovery element. The API instance in API Manager stays 'Unregistered' after deploying to CloudHub. Which causes should be investigated? (Select all that apply)
questions.exhibit:
<api-gateway:autodiscovery
    apiId="${api.id}"
    flowRef="orders-main"/>
options: The api.id property resolves to the WRONG environment's instance id
The environment's Anypoint Platform client_id/client_secret are not configured for the deployment
The flowRef doesn't reference the flow containing the HTTP listener + router
The RAML has a typo in a documentation string
optionNotes: Correct — instance ids are per environment; a dev id in prod pairs with nothing.
Correct — the runtime authenticates to the platform with environment credentials to register.
Correct — autodiscovery must point at the main (routed) flow.
Wrong — spec documentation has no effect on gateway pairing.
questions.explanation: Autodiscovery pairing checklist: correct per-environment api.id + platform client credentials on the runtime + flowRef to the main flow. Miss any and the instance stays Unregistered.

questions.q: A rate-limiting policy must change from 100 to 500 requests/minute on a production API. What is required?
options: Edit the policy on the API instance in API Manager; no redeploy needed
Rebuild the Mule application and redeploy it with the new rate-limit value baked in
Edit the RAML and re-scaffold
Restart the CloudHub workers
optionNotes: Correct — policies are managed configuration: applied, changed, and removed at the instance level and pulled by gateways at runtime.
Wrong — policy config lives outside the application artifact.
Wrong — the spec documents the API; it doesn't carry runtime policy settings.
Wrong — no restart is needed for policy updates.
questions.explanation: Separation of concerns: implementation (deployed artifact) vs governance (API Manager policies). Policy changes are zero-deployment operations.

questions.q:
Order the steps a NEW consumer follows to call a production API governed with client ID enforcement and SLA tiers:
(A) receive client_id/secret  (B) find the API in Exchange  (C) request access with a client app + tier  (D) send credentials on each request
options: B → C → A → D
A → B → C → D
C → B → D → A
D → C → B → A
optionNotes: Correct — discover (Exchange) → request access (client app + tier → contract) → credentials issued → use them per the policy's configured mechanism.
Wrong — credentials don't exist before the access request creates the client app/contract.
Wrong — access is requested against an API you first discover.
Wrong — you cannot call before having credentials.
questions.explanation: The consumer journey: Exchange discovery → access request (tier/contract) → credentials → invocation. The gateway then enforces identity + tier per contract.

questions.q: What does 'mvn clean package' produce for a Mule 4 project, and where?
options: A deployable application JAR in target/ (after MUnit tests run)
A WAR file in dist/
A ZIP in src/main/mule
Nothing is produced unless the -DmuleDeploy flag is supplied to Maven
optionNotes: Correct — package builds the deployable archive (mule-application JAR) into target/, with the test phase running first.
Wrong — Mule 4 artifacts are JARs, not WARs, and output goes to target/.
Wrong — src/ is source; build output never lands there.
Wrong — -DmuleDeploy affects the DEPLOY phase, not packaging.
questions.explanation: mvn clean package = tests + deployable JAR in target/ — the artifact that deploys unchanged to CloudHub, Runtime Fabric, or customer-hosted runtimes.

questions.q: Which platform component and mechanism HIDE a sensitive property value that operators set on a CloudHub application?
options: Hidden Runtime Manager properties, plus secureProperties in mule-artifact.json
Encrypting the packaged JAR file so property values cannot be read from it at rest
Exchange access controls
Setting the property in the RAML
optionNotes: Correct — deployment properties can be hidden in Runtime Manager; secureProperties in mule-artifact.json masks named keys from display.
Wrong — artifact encryption isn't a platform feature and wouldn't affect property display.
Wrong — Exchange governs assets, not runtime properties.
Wrong — specs don't carry deployment configuration.
questions.explanation: Sensitive deploy-time values: set them as hidden Runtime Manager properties and/or list keys in mule-artifact.json's secureProperties so the platform masks them.

questions.q: A consumer sends the request shown and receives the response shown. Which statement is correct?
questions.exhibit:
REQUEST
POST /v1/orders HTTP/1.1
Host: api.acme.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json

{ "sku": "A320", "qty": 2 }

RESPONSE
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /v1/orders/8842
Content-Type: application/json

{ "id": 8842, "status": "NEW" }
options: The client asked for XML but the server ignored the Accept header
201 means the server accepted the request but has not created anything yet
A new order resource was created and its URI is given in the Location header
The request is invalid because POST must not carry a body
questions.explanation: 201 Created is the standard response to a successful POST that creates a resource; the new resource's URI is returned in the Location header (/v1/orders/8842). Content-Type states the body's format; Accept requested JSON, which the server honored.
optionNotes: Wrong — Accept was application/json and the response is JSON, so the header was honored.
Wrong — 201 means the resource was created; 202 Accepted is the code for 'accepted, not yet processed'.
Correct — 201 Created plus a Location header pointing at /v1/orders/8842 is the textbook create-resource response.
Wrong — POST routinely carries a body; that is how the new resource's data is sent.

questions.q: A team wants every method in the API to document the same 404 and 429 error responses without repeating the block. Which RAML construct fits, and how is it applied?
questions.exhibit:
traits:
  standardErrors:
    responses:
      404:
        body:
          application/json:
            example: { "error": "Not found" }
      429:
        body:
          application/json:
            example: { "error": "Rate limit exceeded" }

/flights:
  get:
    is: [ ??? ]
  /{id}:
    get:
      is: [ ??? ]
options: A resourceType, applied to each resource with 'type: standardErrors'
A trait, applied to each method with 'is: [standardErrors]'
A securityScheme, applied with 'securedBy: [standardErrors]'
A DataType fragment, applied with 'type: standardErrors'
questions.explanation: Reusable method-level declarations (here, common error responses) are a trait, applied to a method with 'is: [traitName]'. The '???' placeholders sit under get: methods, confirming method-level application.
optionNotes: Wrong — a resourceType shapes a whole resource and is applied with 'type:' on the resource, not on each method.
Correct — the block declares responses (method-level) and the placeholders are under get: methods, so it is a trait applied with 'is:'.
Wrong — securitySchemes describe authentication, not shared error responses.
Wrong — a DataType defines a data structure; it cannot carry method responses.

questions.q: A Scatter-Gather calls two airline systems, then a Transform runs the script shown on its output. Given the aggregated payload, what does the script produce?
questions.explanation: pluck collects each route's .payload into an array of arrays; flatten merges them into a single array of flight objects. The keys ('0','1') are dropped by pluck, and the objects are preserved (not reduced to strings).
optionNotes: Correct — pluck yields [[UA1],[DL2,DL9]] and flatten merges to one array of the three objects.
Wrong — that is the input shape; pluck+flatten specifically removes the index keys.
Wrong — that is the result of pluck alone; flatten collapses the outer nesting.
Wrong — the objects keep their structure; nothing projects them down to the 'f' string.

questions.q: A developer sees these two log lines for one request. What is the most likely problem, and where should they look first?
questions.exhibit:
INFO  2026-01-05 10:22:31,441 [http-listener.01] [event: a1b2c3]
      ...LoggerMessageProcessor: Incoming order for customer 8842
ERROR 2026-01-05 10:22:31,455 [http-listener.01] [event: a1b2c3]
      ...DefaultMessagingExceptionStrategy:
      HTTP:NOT_FOUND, GET http://crm/customers/8842 returned 404
options: The application failed to start; check the port configuration in the HTTP Listener
The CRM lookup for customer 8842 returned 404; check the request path and whether the record exists
The two lines are unrelated because they came from different requests
A DataWeave type mismatch occurred; check the Transform's output MIME type
questions.explanation: Both lines share the same correlation id (event: a1b2c3), so they trace one request: an HTTP Request to the CRM for customer 8842 returned 404, raising HTTP:NOT_FOUND. Look at the request path/existence of the record — and consider mapping HTTP:NOT_FOUND to a business error.
optionNotes: Wrong — the app is clearly running; it logged and then failed on a downstream call.
Correct — same correlation id, and the error is a 404 from the CRM customer lookup.
Wrong — the identical [event: a1b2c3] id proves they're the same request.
Wrong — the error type is HTTP:NOT_FOUND, not a DataWeave/type error.

sections.title: Explaining Application Network Basics
objectives: Explain MuleSoft's proposal for closing the IT delivery gap
Describe the characteristics and benefits of modern APIs and API-led connectivity
Describe the role of a Center for Enablement (C4E)
Define the basic elements of HTTP requests and responses
Describe the capabilities and high-level components of Anypoint Platform
sections.notes:
The IT delivery gap

Business demand on IT grows every year (new channels, SaaS apps, mobile, IoT), but IT delivery capacity stays roughly flat when every project is a custom point-to-point integration. Point-to-point creates tight coupling: every new connection is bespoke code, changes ripple unpredictably, and nothing is reusable. The gap between what the business asks for and what IT can deliver keeps widening.

MuleSoft's proposal: build an application network — a network of applications, data, and devices connected with reusable, discoverable, self-serve APIs. Key properties of an application network:

  • It is built incrementally, project by project ("bendable, not breakable"). Each project consumes existing APIs and contributes new ones, so delivery gets faster over time.
  • Nodes are loosely coupled: consumers depend on API contracts, not implementations, so implementations can change without breaking consumers.
  • Assets are discoverable and self-serve through Anypoint Exchange — teams find and reuse instead of rebuilding.
  • It is recomposable: existing APIs are rewired into new products and channels quickly.

API-led connectivity — the three layers

  • System APIs — mediate access to a core system (one system, canonical data model). They hide complexity (SOAP, JDBC, proprietary formats) and shield consumers from system changes/migrations.
  • Process APIs — encapsulate business processes that interact with and shape data across systems (e.g. "create order" touching CRM + ERP). They are independent of both the source systems and the delivery channels.
  • Experience APIs — reconfigure data so it is easily consumed by its intended audience/channel, without duplicating process logic per channel.

Exam tip: given a scenario ("an API that aggregates customer data from three systems for a mobile app"), be able to place each API in the correct layer. Aggregation/orchestration → Process; channel-specific shaping → Experience; direct system unlock → System.

Modern APIs

MuleSoft describes modern APIs as having three characteristics: they are treated as products (designed for and marketed to consumers, with a lifecycle), they are discoverable and self-describing (documentation, specs, examples in Exchange), and they are managed (secured, monitored, governed with policies and SLAs).

Center for Enablement (C4E)

A C4E is a cross-functional team (IT + line-of-business members) that enables the rest of the organization instead of delivering everything itself:

  • Builds and publishes reusable assets (APIs, templates, examples, best-practice guides) as products.
  • Establishes and evangelizes best practices; supports and trains project teams.
  • Measures consumption of assets (KPIs like reuse rate) — the network's value grows with reuse.

Contrast with a traditional Center of Excellence (CoE): a CoE centralizes delivery ("do it for the teams"), which becomes a bottleneck; a C4E decentralizes delivery and centralizes enablement.

HTTP fundamentals

MethodPurposeHas body?Idempotent?
GETRetrieve a resource/collectionNoYes (safe)
POSTCreate a resource / process dataYesNo
PUTReplace a resource at a known URI (create-or-replace)YesYes
PATCHPartially update a resourceYesNo (in general)
DELETERemove a resourceNoYes

An HTTP request = method + URI (+ query string) + protocol version + headers + optional body. An HTTP response = status line + headers + optional body. Common status codes:

CodeMeaning
200 / 201 / 202 / 204OK / Created / Accepted (async) / No Content
301 / 304Moved Permanently / Not Modified
400 / 401 / 403 / 404 / 405 / 415 / 429Bad Request / Unauthorized (not authenticated) / Forbidden (not authorized) / Not Found / Method Not Allowed / Unsupported Media Type / Too Many Requests (rate limit)
500 / 502 / 503 / 504Internal Server Error / Bad Gateway / Service Unavailable / Gateway Timeout

Anypoint Platform components

AreaComponentWhat it does
DesignDesign Center (API Designer)Web tool to write API specifications (RAML/OAS) and build fragments; includes the mocking service
Anypoint Studio / Code BuilderDesktop/cloud IDEs to implement, test, and debug Mule applications
Share & discoverAnypoint ExchangeCatalog of reusable assets: specs, fragments, connectors, templates, examples, custom policies; generates API portals and REST Connect connectors
Manage (control plane)API ManagerCreate/manage API instances, apply policies, SLA tiers, contracts, alerts, proxies
Runtime ManagerDeploy, manage, scale, and monitor applications on any deployment target
Access ManagementUsers, roles, permissions, business groups, environments, connected apps
Anypoint Monitoring / VisualizerDashboards, logs, alerts / real-time visualization of the application network
Run (runtime plane)Mule runtimeRuns apps on CloudHub (MuleSoft-hosted), Runtime Fabric (containers), or customer-hosted servers

Exam tip: know which component does what. "Where do consumers discover and request access to an API?" → Exchange. "Where are policies applied?" → API Manager. "Where do you scale workers?" → Runtime Manager.

Point-to-point vs. the application network

The problem an application network solves is combinatorial. With point-to-point integration, connecting n systems that all need to talk to each other trends toward n·(n−1)/2 bespoke connections; every new system multiplies the work, and every change to one endpoint can break many hand-coded links. An API-led approach inverts this: each system is unlocked once by a System API, and everything else composes those reusable contracts, so the marginal cost of the next project falls instead of rising.

Because consumers bind to contracts (the API specification) rather than to implementations, a System API can be re-pointed from a legacy database to a new SaaS backend without any consumer changing a line of code — the coupling lives at the contract, which stays stable.

Benefits of an application network

BenefitWhy it follows from API-led connectivity
Faster delivery over timeEach project reuses existing APIs and contributes new ones, so the next project starts further ahead ("every project delivered faster than the last").
Decentralized, self-serve accessTeams discover and consume assets in Exchange without filing tickets against a central team.
Increased operational resilienceLoose coupling and clear contracts localize the blast radius of change and failure.
Visibility and governanceTraffic flows through managed APIs, so it can be secured, throttled, monitored, and analyzed centrally.
Reuse instead of rebuildA System API written once is consumed by many Process and Experience APIs — the network effect that closes the delivery gap.

REST principles

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style, not a protocol. The properties MCD Level 1 expects you to recognize:

  • Resources addressed by URIs — nouns, not verbs: /flights, /flights/A320. The HTTP method carries the verb.
  • Uniform interface — the same small set of methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) works across all resources with consistent semantics.
  • Statelessness — every request carries everything the server needs; the server keeps no client session between calls. This is what lets you scale horizontally by adding identical workers behind a load balancer.
  • Representations — the same resource can be returned as JSON, XML, etc.; the client negotiates format with the Accept header and declares its body's format with Content-Type.
  • Cacheability & safe/idempotent methods — GET is safe (no side effects) and cacheable; PUT and DELETE are idempotent (repeating them lands the same state); POST is neither.

REST vs. SOAP web services

RESTSOAP
StyleArchitectural style over HTTPStrict protocol (envelope) over HTTP, JMS, etc.
ContractRAML / OAS (optional but recommended)WSDL (required)
PayloadUsually JSON (also XML, form, binary)Always XML SOAP envelope
Consumed in Mule viaHTTP Request / REST ConnectorWeb Service Consumer connector

On the exam, "generated WSDL / Web Service Consumer / envelope" signals SOAP; "RAML, resources, JSON, HTTP methods" signals REST.

API as a product and its lifecycle

A modern API is treated as a product with a full lifecycle rather than a one-off endpoint. The design-first lifecycle Anypoint supports:

Design → simulate (mocking service) → gather consumer feedback → refine the contract; only then implement, deploy, and manage. The point is that consumers validate the contract before anyone writes a flow.

Control plane vs. runtime plane

Anypoint Platform separates where you manage from where your apps run:

  • Control plane — the management layer: Design Center, Exchange, API Manager, Runtime Manager, Access Management, Monitoring. Hosted by MuleSoft (or, for Anypoint Platform Private Cloud Edition, self-hosted).
  • Runtime plane — where Mule runtimes actually execute your apps: CloudHub / CloudHub 2.0 (MuleSoft-hosted iPaaS), Runtime Fabric (your containers/Kubernetes, on-prem or in your cloud), or customer-hosted standalone Mule servers.

Exam tip: "apply a policy / create an SLA tier" → API Manager (control plane). "restart or scale workers" → Runtime Manager (control plane) acting on the runtime plane. "the app processes a message" → Mule runtime (runtime plane).

sections.title: Designing and Consuming APIs
objectives: Describe the lifecycle of the modern API (design, simulate, feedback, validate)
Use RAML to define API resources, nested resources, and methods
Identify when to use query parameters vs URI parameters
Use RAML to define request/response bodies, headers, and status codes
Use RAML to define reusable data types and format-independent examples
Formulate RESTful requests from a RAML API specification
sections.notes:
The design-first API lifecycle

Modern APIs are designed before they are implemented: design → simulate → get feedback → validate, iterating until consumers agree the contract is right. Only then is the API implemented, tested, deployed, and managed. Tools per stage:

  • Design — API Designer (Design Center) writing RAML or OAS.
  • Simulate — the mocking service serves responses straight from the spec's examples, giving consumers a live endpoint before any code exists.
  • Feedback — publish to Exchange; consumers explore the auto-generated API console/portal and comment.
  • Validate — refine the spec until it is stable; it becomes the contract that APIkit later enforces.

RAML essentials

#%RAML 1.0
title: American Flights API
version: v1
baseUri: http://localhost:8081/api

types:
  AmericanFlight: !include dataTypes/american-flight.raml

/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      destination:
        required: false
        enum: [SFO, LAX, CLE]
    responses:
      200:
        body:
          application/json:
            type: AmericanFlight[]
            examples: !include examples/AmericanFlightsExample.raml
  post:
    body:
      application/json:
        type: AmericanFlight
    responses:
      201:
        body:
          application/json:
            example: {"message": "Flight added"}
  /{ID}:
    get:
      responses:
        200:
          body:
            application/json:
              type: AmericanFlight
    put:
    delete:
  • RAML is YAML-based: indentation defines nesting, and it is case-sensitive.
  • Resources begin with /; nesting a resource under another creates a sub-path (/flights/{ID}).
  • URI parameters use {curlyBraces} and identify a specific resource. Query parameters are declared under a method's queryParameters: and filter/sort/paginate collections. Rule of thumb: identity → URI parameter; refinement of a result set → query parameter.
  • Each method can declare headers:, body: (per media type), and responses: (per status code, each with its own body/type/example).

Reuse in RAML

MechanismScopeExample use
types: / DataType fragmentData structurestype: AmericanFlight shared by requests and responses
NamedExample fragmentExample instancesOne example file reused by several methods (format-independent)
traits: (applied with is:)Method-level patternsPagination query params, standard error responses, auth headers
resourceTypes: (applied with type:)Resource-level patternsStandard "collection" and "member" resource shapes
Library (uses:)Bundle of types/traits/etc.Common enterprise definitions shared across specs
Overlay / ExtensionWhole specAdd annotations/translations (overlay) or environment additions (extension) without touching the master spec

Fragments (#%RAML 1.0 DataType, Trait, NamedExample…) can live in separate files included with !include, or be published to Exchange as reusable assets and imported into many API specs as dependencies.

A RAML data type defines fields, types, and constraints:

#%RAML 1.0 DataType
type: object
properties:
  ID: integer
  code:
    type: string
    pattern: "[A-Z]{3}\\d{3}"
  price:
    type: number
    required: false

Consuming APIs

  • To call an API from a spec: match method + full path (baseUri + resource) + required parameters/headers/body. E.g. from the spec above: GET http://localhost:8081/api/flights?destination=SFO or POST /api/flights with a JSON AmericanFlight body.
  • When a spec is published to Exchange, REST Connect automatically generates a REST Connector (one operation per method) that can be dragged into a Mule flow — an alternative to configuring raw HTTP Request operations.
  • The API console (in Design Center or Exchange) and the mocking service let anyone try requests without an implementation.

Exam tip: expect questions that show a RAML snippet and ask which request is valid (correct path nesting, query vs URI parameter, required vs optional). Read the indentation carefully — it defines what belongs to what.

RAML data types and facets

Types are the heart of a RAML contract. A type has a base (object, array, string, number, integer, boolean, date-only, datetime, file, or another named type) plus facets that constrain values. APIkit validates incoming requests against these facets and returns 400 when they fail.

FacetApplies toMeaning
requiredpropertyPresent by default; name?: or required: false makes it optional
enumscalarValue must be one of a fixed list
patternstringValue must match the regex
minLength / maxLengthstringLength bounds
minimum / maximumnumberValue bounds
defaultanyValue used when the property is omitted
example / examplestypeSample instance(s); feed the console and mocking service
#%RAML 1.0 DataType
type: object
properties:
  id:      integer
  email:
    type: string
    pattern: ".+@.+"
  tier:
    enum: [bronze, silver, gold]
    default: bronze
  nickname?: string          # optional (the ? suffix)

Composite types: Flight[] is an array of Flight; string | number is a union; a type can also extend another via type: BaseType and add properties.

Securing an API in RAML: securitySchemes

A spec can declare how it is secured so the contract, docs, and generated connector all know. Common schemes: OAuth 2.0, Basic Authentication, Pass Through, and custom x-{name} schemes such as client-id enforcement.

securitySchemes:
  clientId:
    type: Pass Through
    describedBy:
      headers:
        client_id:     { required: true }
        client_secret: { required: true }

/orders:
  get:
    securedBy: [clientId]

Declaring a scheme documents the requirement; the actual enforcement at runtime is done by an API Manager policy (e.g. Client ID Enforcement, OAuth 2.0 access-token validation). Contract in RAML, enforcement in API Manager — a frequent exam distinction.

Status codes an API declares

Each method lists the responses it can return, per status code, each with its own body/type/example. A well-designed API declares its success and error responses so consumers can code against them:

SituationTypical status
Read succeeded200 OK
Resource created (POST)201 Created (+ Location header)
Accepted for async processing202 Accepted
Validation / malformed request400 Bad Request
Missing/invalid credentials401 Unauthorized
Authenticated but not allowed403 Forbidden
Unknown resource404 Not Found
Method not declared on the resource405 Method Not Allowed
Rate limit exceeded429 Too Many Requests

The mocking service in depth

Toggle "Mocking service" in API Designer and the platform stands up a live endpoint that answers requests using the spec's declared examples — no implementation required. This is what makes design-first real: consumers send actual HTTP calls, see representative payloads, and give feedback on the contract before a flow exists. The mock honors declared status codes and validates parameters just like the real APIkit router will.

Consuming an API from a Mule app

  • HTTP Request connector — configure method, host/port/path, headers, query params, and body by hand. Works for any HTTP API.
  • REST Connector (REST Connect) — when a spec is published to Exchange, a typed connector with one operation per method is generated automatically; drag it in from Exchange for a higher-level, contract-aware call.
  • Web Service Consumer — for SOAP services described by a WSDL (not REST).

Exam tip: "publish a RAML and get a draggable connector" → REST Connect. "call a SOAP service" → Web Service Consumer. "call any REST endpoint manually" → HTTP Request.

sections.title: Accessing and Modifying Mule Events
objectives: Describe the Mule event data structure (message payload, attributes, variables)
Use transformers to set the payload, attributes, and variables
Write DataWeave expressions to access and modify payload, attributes, and variables
Enrich Mule events using target parameters
sections.notes:
Anatomy of a Mule event

  • Payload — the body/data being processed. Can be any type: JSON, XML, Java object, stream…
  • Attributes — metadata that arrived with the message. For an HTTP Listener: attributes.queryParams, attributes.uriParams, attributes.headers, attributes.method, attributes.requestPath. For a File listener: file name, size, timestamps.
  • Variables (vars) — data you attach to the event; they live for the rest of the flow and travel through Flow References.

The Mule event is immutable — every change creates a new event instance behind the scenes. Practically, this means each processor receives the event produced by the previous one.

The golden rule of attributes

Every time the event passes through a connector operation (HTTP Request, Database Select, File Read…), the message is replaced: the payload becomes the operation's result AND the attributes are replaced with metadata about that result (e.g. HTTP response status and headers). If you need the original query params later, save them to a variable first.

Accessing event data with DataWeave

payload                              // whole payload
payload.customer.lastName            // field navigation
payload[0].price                     // array index
attributes.queryParams.destination   // ?destination=SFO
attributes.uriParams.ID              // /flights/{ID}
attributes.headers.'client_id'       // request header
vars.myVar                           // a variable
vars.order.items[0]                  // variables hold any structure
correlationId                        // event correlation ID
message                              // the whole message (payload+attributes)

Inline expressions inside component fields use #[ ... ], e.g. a Logger message of #['Got flight ' ++ attributes.uriParams.ID]. Property placeholders (${http.port}) are resolved at startup and are NOT DataWeave.

Setting event data

ComponentSetsNotes
Transform Messagepayload, any variable, attributesFull DataWeave script per output; the primary transformation tool
Set PayloadpayloadValue field accepts literals or #[expressions]; can set MIME type
Set Variableone variableName + value (literal or expression)
Remove Variable—Deletes a variable from the event

Enrichment with target parameters

Most connector operations expose target and target value parameters. Setting target = flightData (and optionally targetValue = #[payload.data]) stores the operation's result in vars.flightData and leaves the current payload untouched — the Mule 4 replacement for Mule 3's message enricher. Use it whenever you must combine "what I have" with "what another system returns".

Exam tip: after an HTTP Request with target=response, the payload is unchanged and vars.response holds the response body. Without a target, payload = response body and attributes = response metadata. Both facts are heavily tested.

Attributes by event source

Attributes are strongly typed to whatever produced the message, so the same attributes. prefix exposes very different fields depending on the source or the last operation:

Source / operationUseful attributes
HTTP Listenermethod, requestPath, queryParams, uriParams, headers, queryString
HTTP Request (response)statusCode, reasonPhrase, headers
On New / Updated File, File ReadfileName, path, size, creationTime, lastModifiedTime
Database Select(payload holds rows; attributes are minimal)
JMS / VM / Anypoint MQmessage id, correlation id, delivery/redelivery metadata

This is why the golden rule matters: a Logger placed after an HTTP Request sees attributes.statusCode, not the original attributes.queryParams — those were replaced. Capture them in a variable before the operation if you still need them.

Payload MIME type and encoding

A payload carries not just bytes but a MIME type (e.g. application/json) and encoding. DataWeave reads the input based on that MIME type, so setting it correctly matters: if an HTTP body arrives as text/plain but is really JSON, payload.field won't navigate until you tell Mule it's JSON. Set the output type in a Transform Message's output directive, or on Set Payload via its MIME-type option. Mismatched types are a common cause of "expected object but got string" errors.

Variable scope and lifetime

  • A variable set with Set Variable lives for the rest of the flow and travels into flows called via Flow Reference — the called flow sees and can modify the same variables.
  • Variables do not automatically cross an HTTP boundary: calling another app over HTTP sends only payload/headers, not your vars.
  • Because the event is immutable, "changing" a variable actually produces a new event; within a single flow you simply read the latest value.
  • Remove Variable deletes one; there is no implicit clearing of variables between processors.

Exam tip: a Flow Reference shares variables with the parent; an HTTP Request does not. Given a diagram where a sub-flow sets vars.x, the caller can read vars.x afterward only if it was a Flow Reference, not an HTTP call.

sections.title: Structuring Mule Applications
objectives: Parameterize an application using property placeholders
Define and reuse global configurations in an application
Break an application into multiple flows using flow references, private flows, and subflows
Specify what data is persisted between flows and across a flow reference
Describe the purpose of domain projects
sections.notes:
Project structure

my-app/
├── pom.xml                     <- Maven build (dependencies, plugins)
├── mule-artifact.json          <- app descriptor (minMuleVersion, secureProperties)
└── src/
    ├── main/
    │   ├── mule/               <- Mule configuration XMLs (flows, global elements)
    │   └── resources/          <- properties files, DW modules, examples, log4j2.xml
    └── test/
        ├── munit/              <- MUnit test suites
        └── resources/

Property placeholders

Externalize anything environment-specific (hosts, ports, credentials, paths):

# src/main/resources/config/dev.yaml
db:
  host: "dev-db.acme.com"
  port: "3306"
http:
  listener:
    port: "8081"
  • Register the file with a Configuration properties global element. Parameterize the file name itself to switch environments: config/${env}.yaml, then run with -Denv=dev (in Studio: Run Configuration → VM arguments -Denv=dev -M-Denv=dev pattern for Maven).
  • Reference values as ${db.host} in any configuration field, or p('db.host') inside DataWeave.
  • Precedence: deployment/system properties (-D, Runtime Manager properties) override file values — this is how the same JAR runs in every environment.
  • Secrets should use secure configuration properties (encrypted) — covered in depth in MCD L2, but know they exist and use the secure:: prefix.

Global configurations

Connector configurations (HTTP Listener config, HTTP Request config, Database config…) are global elements: defined once, referenced by any number of operations. Best practice is to keep them in a dedicated config file (e.g. global.xml) so they're easy to find and reuse across the app's XML files — all files are combined at deployment.

Flows, private flows, subflows

FlowPrivate flowSubflow
Event sourceYes (optional but typical)NoNo
Own error handlingYesYesNo — inherits caller's
Invoked bySource or Flow ReferenceFlow ReferenceFlow Reference
Performance——Slightly faster (no extra context)
Callable from DataWeave lookup()YesYesNo

A Flow Reference passes the entire event (payload + attributes + variables) into the referenced flow synchronously. Changes made there (payload, new variables) return to the caller. This is fundamentally different from crossing a transport boundary:

  • App A calls App B over HTTP → only the payload (and headers you set) cross; variables and attributes do NOT.
  • App B receives a brand-new event with its own attributes (from B's HTTP Listener) and empty vars.

Exam tip: "A flow sets var X then calls another flow in the same app via Flow Reference — is X visible?" YES. "…calls another Mule app via HTTP Request — is X visible there?" NO.

Async scope

The Async scope runs its processors in a separate thread and the flow continues immediately — use it for fire-and-forget work (audit logging, notifications) that shouldn't delay the response. The async branch gets a copy of the event; its changes don't come back.

Domain projects

A Mule domain is a special project that holds shared global configurations. Applications deployed to the same customer-hosted runtime can reference one domain and share, e.g., a single HTTP Listener config — meaning several apps share one port. Domains reduce duplicated config and resource usage, but: not supported on CloudHub (each CloudHub app runs isolated on its own worker), all apps must be deployed alongside the domain, and redeploying the domain restarts its apps.

The application descriptor (mule-artifact.json)

Every Mule 4 app has a mule-artifact.json at its root describing the deployable artifact to the runtime:

{
  "minMuleVersion": "4.4.0",
  "secureProperties": ["db.password", "api.token"],
  "configs": ["global.xml", "order-api.xml"],
  "redeploymentEnabled": true
}
  • minMuleVersion — the runtime refuses to deploy on an older version.
  • secureProperties — names of properties to keep encrypted/hidden (their values live in secure config).
  • Build settings (dependencies, packaging) live in pom.xml; the descriptor is about the runtime artifact, not the Maven build.

Splitting configuration across files

All XML files under src/main/mule/ are combined into one application at deploy time, so you are free to organize by concern: global.xml for connector configs and property placeholders, one file per API/flow group, error.xml for a shared error handler. Flow Reference, global-config references, and error-handler references all resolve across files. This keeps large apps navigable without changing runtime behavior.

Choosing flow, sub-flow, or private flow

NeedUse
An entry point triggered by a source (HTTP, Scheduler…)Flow (with a source)
Reusable logic that must run under the caller's error handling and transactionSub-flow
Reusable logic that needs its own error handlingPrivate flow
Fire-and-forget work that must not delay the responseAsync scope
Callable from DataWeave lookup()Flow or private flow (never a sub-flow)

Exam tip: the giveaway for sub-flow vs private flow is error handling and transactions — a sub-flow is inlined into the caller (shares both); a private flow is its own processing scope (its own error handler).

sections.title: Building API Implementation Interfaces
objectives: Manually create a RESTful interface for a Mule application
Generate a REST Connector from a RAML specification
Describe the features and benefits of APIkit
Use APIkit to create implementation flows from a RAML file
Describe how requests are routed through flows generated by APIkit
sections.notes:
Manual RESTful interfaces

You can hand-build an interface: one HTTP Listener per resource/method (paths like /flights/{ID}, listener metadata gives attributes.uriParams), or one listener with wildcard path (/*) plus Choice routers on attributes.requestPath and attributes.method. It works, but you must implement validation, error responses, and routing yourself — and nothing guarantees you stay in sync with the API specification. That is the problem APIkit solves.

APIkit

APIkit is an open-source toolkit that scaffolds a Mule application from an API specification (RAML or OAS; newer versions also support AsyncAPI, GraphQL, OData, SOAP via APIkit for SOAP). Import the spec from Exchange or the filesystem into a Mule project, and scaffolding generates:

  • The main flow: HTTP Listener (default path /api/*) → APIkit Router, plus an error handler with mappings for the standard APIkit errors.
  • One implementation flow per resource/method pair, named by strict convention: get:\flights:american-flights-api-config, get:\flights\(ID):american-flights-api-config, post:\flights:application\json:american-flights-api-config (the media type appears when a request body is declared). The flow name is the routing contract — never rename it.
  • A console flow exposing the interactive API console (default path /console/*).

How the APIkit Router works

  1. Validates each incoming request against the specification: path exists, method allowed, required query params/headers present, body matches the declared type.
  2. Routes valid requests to the implementation flow whose name matches the method + resource (+ media type) + router config name.
  3. Populates attributes.uriParams and attributes.queryParams.
  4. Serializes the flow's output as the HTTP response; sets the status code (200 by default, or whatever the flow sets in attributes / the listener's response builder).

Validation failures raise typed errors mapped by the generated error handler:

APIkit errorHTTP status
APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST400
APIKIT:NOT_FOUND404
APIKIT:METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED405
APIKIT:NOT_ACCEPTABLE406
APIKIT:UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE415
APIKIT:NOT_IMPLEMENTED501

Exam tip: a request for a resource that exists in the spec but has no implementation flow yet → the scaffolded flow returns the spec's example (if scaffolded with examples) — while a path that isn't in the spec at all → APIKIT:NOT_FOUND → 404. A missing required query param → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST → 400.

Keeping spec and implementation in sync

  • Update the RAML → right-click the API in Studio → Scaffold flows from these API specifications: new resource/method pairs get new flows; existing implementation flows are preserved.
  • The spec can be kept as a design library dependency from Exchange, so implementation always tracks the published contract version.

REST Connect connectors

When an API specification is published to Exchange, the platform auto-generates a REST Connector (REST Connect). Consumers add it from Exchange to their Mule project and get one typed operation per method — with fields for parameters and metadata for DataSense — instead of hand-configuring HTTP Request URLs. If the connector can't be generated, consumers can still download the spec or call the API with plain HTTP Request.

The generated flow naming convention

APIkit routes by flow name, so the names it scaffolds are a contract, not decoration. The pattern is method:\resource(:mediaType):routerConfigName:

Spec resource + methodGenerated flow name
GET /flightsget:\flights:api-config
GET /flights/{ID}get:\flights\(ID):api-config
POST /flights (JSON body)post:\flights:application\json:api-config

The media-type segment (application\json) appears only when the method declares a request body, which lets one resource have different flows per content type. Rename any of these and the router raises APIKIT:NOT_IMPLEMENTED (501) for that operation.

The APIkit main flow and error mappings

Scaffolding produces a main flow plus an error handler that turns each APIkit validation error into the right HTTP status. Conceptually:

<flow name="api-main">
  <http:listener path="/api/*" config-ref="api-httpListenerConfig"/>
  <apikit:router config-ref="api-config"/>
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-propagate type="APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST">
      <set-variable variableName="httpStatus" value="400"/>
      <set-payload value='#[{ "error": error.description }]'/>
    </on-error-propagate>
    <on-error-propagate type="APIKIT:NOT_FOUND">   ... 404 ...
    <on-error-propagate type="APIKIT:METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED"> ... 405 ...
  </error-handler>
</flow>

You customize these mappings (payload shape, extra logging) but keep the types aligned with the router's errors.

Manual routing vs APIkit

Manual (Listener + Choice)APIkit
RoutingYou write Choice on path/methodGenerated from the spec, by flow name
ValidationYou implement itAutomatic against the RAML/OAS
Error responsesHand-builtScaffolded mappings (400/404/405/…)
Stays in sync with specNo guaranteeRe-scaffold preserves your flows

Exam tip: choose APIkit whenever the scenario mentions a RAML/OAS spec and "consistent with the contract" or "validate automatically"; choose manual only for trivial or non-spec endpoints.

sections.title: Routing Events
objectives: Use the Choice router to route events based on conditional logic
Use the Scatter-Gather router to multicast events
Validate data using the Validation module
sections.notes:
Choice router — conditional, exactly one route

<choice>
